sessions.title: Geographic Thinking and Map Relationships
sessions.anchor: What, where, why; absolute and relative location; site and situation; scale; India relationship drills
sessions.watch: Learn how geography asks why here and not there, then use location, scale, site, situation, and map relationships to read India spatially.
sessions.talk: Why is asking why here and not there more useful than memorizing isolated locations? Explain with one India map relationship.
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: geographic lens, absolute-relative location, site-situation, scale, and India relationship drills.
sessions.track: Mark whether you can explain one location through relationship, scale, and an India map cue.
sessions.revisit: Rebuild one India map relationship using what, where, why, site, situation, and scale.
sessions.gsCompatibility: GS Paper I Geography foundation: spatial distribution, location logic, physical-human relationships, and applied India map reading.

sessions.title: Universe, Solar System, and Origin of Earth
sessions.anchor: Big Bang, expansion of space, CMB, redshift, galaxies, solar nebula, accretion, differentiation, atmosphere, hydrosphere
sessions.watch: Study the Universe sample module as short slides, then connect cosmic evidence with Earth formation and habitability.
sessions.talk: Explain the Universe-to-Earth chain: Big Bang evidence, expanding space, solar nebula, accretion, differentiation, and one UPSC trap.
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: Big Bang evidence, redshift, CMB, solar nebula, accretion, differentiation, atmosphere and hydrosphere.
sessions.track: Separate the causal sequence from disconnected universe facts and examiner traps.
sessions.revisit: Speak the eight-step chain: expansion, evidence, gravity, solar nebula, accretion, differentiation, atmosphere, hydrosphere.
sessions.gsCompatibility: GS Paper I Physical Geography foundation: origin of the universe, solar-system formation, evolution of Earth, differentiation, atmosphere, hydrosphere, and statement-trap logic.

sessions.title: Interior of Earth, Seismology, and Plate Movement
sessions.anchor: Interior of Earth, seismic waves, crust, mantle, core, lithosphere, asthenosphere, convection, plate boundaries
sessions.watch: Use seismic evidence to read Earth layers, then connect mantle behavior with moving plates and hazard patterns.
sessions.talk: How do seismic waves and mantle dynamics explain why earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountains are not randomly distributed?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: Earth layers, seismic evidence, convection and plate boundaries.
sessions.track: Map weak links between layers, wave behavior, convection and boundary types.
sessions.revisit: Redraw Earth layers, then compare convergent, divergent, and transform boundaries.

sessions.title: Volcanoes, Rocks, and Geomorphic Processes
sessions.anchor: Volcanoes, rocks and rock cycle, weathering, mass wasting, erosion, deposition, landform development
sessions.watch: Understand internal and external forces shaping landforms.
sessions.talk: Why does the same rock produce different landforms in different climates?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: volcanoes, rocks, rock cycle, geomorphic agents and processes.
sessions.track: Tag confusion between process and landform.
sessions.revisit: Make one table of agent, process, and resulting landform.

sessions.title: Atmosphere, Insolation, Pressure, and Winds
sessions.anchor: Atmosphere composition and structure, insolation, heat budget, temperature distribution, pressure belts, winds, jet streams, local winds
sessions.watch: Build pressure and wind logic from unequal heating.
sessions.talk: Why does air move, and why does it curve?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: pressure belts and wind systems.
sessions.track: Check if Coriolis, pressure gradient, and belts are conceptually clear.
sessions.revisit: Sketch global pressure belts without notes.

sessions.title: Humidity, Cyclones, Climate Types, and Ocean System
sessions.anchor: Humidity, precipitation, air masses, fronts, cyclones, anticyclones, climate classification, ocean floor, salinity, temperature, currents, tides
sessions.watch: Relate ocean currents with climate and coastal effects.
sessions.talk: How do moisture, pressure systems, climate classification, and ocean currents connect rainfall, deserts, fog, storms, and fisheries?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: precipitation, fronts, cyclones, ocean currents, tides, and salinity.
sessions.track: Mark cyclone, climate, and current-location pairs for map revision.
sessions.revisit: Trace warm/cold currents and one cyclone pathway on a blank world map.

sessions.title: Indian Monsoon, ENSO-IOD-MJO, and Maritime Zones
sessions.anchor: Indian monsoon, El Nino, La Nina, IOD, MJO, EEZ, continental shelf, maritime zones, physical integration
sessions.watch: Build monsoon as a moving pressure-wind-ocean system, then add climate oscillations and maritime-zone traps.
sessions.talk: Why is the monsoon powerful but unreliable, and how do ENSO, IOD, MJO, and maritime zones create UPSC traps?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: monsoon mechanism, ENSO-IOD-MJO, EEZ, continental shelf, maritime zones.
sessions.track: Generate the first physical geography weak-topic list.
sessions.revisit: Reattempt weak concepts from Days 1-6 before opening India map command.

sessions.title: India Physiography
sessions.anchor: Himalayas, plains, plateau, desert, coast, islands
sessions.watch: Build India relief as a map-first framework.
sessions.talk: How does relief control rivers, climate, soils, agriculture, and risk?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: physiographic divisions.
sessions.track: Map blank zones where recall is weak.
sessions.revisit: Draw India physiographic divisions from memory.

sessions.title: Indian Drainage, Lakes, and Wetlands
sessions.anchor: Drainage overview, Indus, Ganga, Brahmaputra, east-flowing and west-flowing peninsular rivers, lakes, wetlands, Ramsar sites
sessions.watch: Understand drainage through slope, origin, and basin behavior.
sessions.talk: Why do Himalayan and peninsular rivers behave differently?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: rivers and tributaries.
sessions.track: Tag river-origin, tributary, and state-location errors.
sessions.revisit: Trace major rivers and tributaries on a blank map.

sessions.title: Soils, Natural Vegetation, and Forest Policy
sessions.anchor: Soil classification, soil erosion, degradation, forest types, forest cover, conservation, forest policy
sessions.watch: Connect soils and vegetation with climate, parent rock, relief, water, use, and conservation policy.
sessions.talk: Why do soil and vegetation questions often test geography plus environment, and where do UPSC traps appear?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: soils, erosion, forest types, forest cover and policy.
sessions.track: Tag soil-location-crop and forest-climate confusion.
sessions.revisit: Match soil, region, crop, limitation, vegetation, and conservation rule.

sessions.title: Indian Agriculture
sessions.anchor: Cropping seasons, major crops, top producing states, agricultural revolutions, irrigation, food security, agricultural policy, agricultural issues
sessions.watch: Connect crops and irrigation with rainfall, soils, relief, markets, policy, and food security.
sessions.talk: Why do crops cluster where they do, and how do irrigation and food-security policy change the geography?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: cropping seasons, crops/states, revolutions, irrigation, food security, agricultural issues.
sessions.track: Map crop belts, irrigation logic, and policy-linked weak areas.
sessions.revisit: Build one crop-soil-rainfall-state-policy grid.

sessions.title: Minerals, Energy, Industries, and Economic Geography
sessions.anchor: Ferrous, non-ferrous and critical minerals, energy resources, iron and steel, textile industry, industrial corridors
sessions.watch: Connect minerals, energy, and industries with raw material, power, transport, market, policy, and strategic supply chains.
sessions.talk: Why do minerals, energy resources, and industries cluster where they do, and what changes with critical minerals?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: minerals, energy, steel, textile, corridors, and location factors.
sessions.track: Map resource belts, energy regions, and industry-location traps.
sessions.revisit: Compare iron-steel, textile, energy, and critical-mineral location logic.

sessions.title: Transport and Communications
sessions.anchor: Ports and maritime trade, road transport, railway transport, inland waterways, communication corridors
sessions.watch: Study connectivity as a geographic and economic force linking ports, roads, railways, waterways, markets, and regions.
sessions.talk: Why do corridors, ports, roads, railways, and inland waterways transform regions differently?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: ports, maritime trade, roads, railways, inland waterways, corridors.
sessions.track: Map corridor, port, railway, and waterway recall.
sessions.revisit: Trace major transport corridors and port-waterway logic.

sessions.title: World Physical Geography and Map Systems
sessions.anchor: World physiography, world rivers, world lakes, straits and channels, world biomes
sessions.watch: Convert world map facts into cause-location relationships across relief, rivers, lakes, straits, channels, and biomes.
sessions.talk: How can a world map fact become a concept question instead of isolated atlas memory?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: world physiography, rivers, lakes, straits, channels, biomes.
sessions.track: Generate world map weak areas.
sessions.revisit: Repair the weakest five world map locations and biome-climate links.

sessions.title: Human Geography
sessions.anchor: Population geography, migration geography, urban geography, cultural geography
sessions.watch: Study population, migration, urbanization, and culture through resources, economy, risk, networks, and identity.
sessions.talk: Why do people cluster, migrate, urbanize, and form cultural regions in some places and not others?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: population, migration, urban, and cultural geography.
sessions.track: Mark concept gaps in density, growth, migration, urbanization, and cultural regions.
sessions.revisit: Compare density, distribution, demographic transition, migration push-pull, and urban hierarchy.

sessions.title: Indian Political Geography
sessions.anchor: India's borders, Northeast India, tribal geography, strategic regions, political map logic
sessions.watch: Build India's borders, Northeast, and tribal regions through terrain, identity, connectivity, security, and administrative logic.
sessions.talk: Why do border, Northeast, and tribal geography questions require political, physical, and human geography together?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: borders, Northeast, tribal geography, and strategic regional logic.
sessions.track: Separate map recall errors from political-geography reasoning gaps.
sessions.revisit: Map one border region, one Northeast corridor, and one tribal-geography trap.

sessions.title: Current Affairs Geography
sessions.anchor: Geopolitical geography in news, connectivity and infrastructure geography, climate change geography, recent disaster geography
sessions.watch: Attach current news to static geography: geopolitics, connectivity, climate change, disasters, infrastructure, and map-linked policy.
sessions.talk: How should a covered Geography topic unlock a current-affairs hook without turning into random news reading?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: geopolitics, connectivity, climate change, disaster events, and static-current linkages.
sessions.track: Mark news hooks that are unlocked by covered static topics.
sessions.revisit: Turn one current event into cause, location, impact, institution, and UPSC trap.

sessions.title: Integrated Physical and India Map Recall
sessions.anchor: Physical geography, India map, monsoon, drainage, soils, resources, agriculture, world map integration
sessions.watch: Run a cumulative recall circuit that links physical drivers with India and world map evidence.
sessions.talk: Can you connect relief, climate, rivers, soils, resources, agriculture, and world map facts as one system?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: integrated physical, India map, and world map drill.
sessions.track: Build the full weak-area heatmap before mock practice.
sessions.revisit: Re-explain every weak Day 1-17 concept before adding final PYQ load.

sessions.title: Weak-Area Repair and PYQ Mock
sessions.anchor: Personalized recovery, PYQ pattern reading, statement traps, atlas repair, mixed mock, error analysis
sessions.watch: Repair only the tracked weak zones, then read PYQ traps and run a mixed mock.
sessions.talk: What is the root cause of each repeated mistake: knowledge gap, map confusion, statement trap, or confidence error?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: weak-area retest, PYQ-style mixed mock, and map repair.
sessions.track: Compare original and retest confidence.
sessions.revisit: Schedule remaining weak topics into the final command-day revision queue.

sessions.title: Geography Command Day
sessions.anchor: Final recall, maps, formulas, examples, PYQ traps, confidence audit, revision lock
sessions.watch: Close the subject with fast full-syllabus recall, map confidence, PYQ traps, and revision scheduling.
sessions.talk: Can every major Geography theme be explained through cause, location, map proof, and one UPSC trap?
sessions.test: Fresh MCQ slot: command-day test and final error classification.
sessions.track: Mark Geography as launch-ready, revision-needed, or retest-needed.
sessions.revisit: Lock the next revision dates.

lessons.title: Geographic Thinking and Map Relationships
lessons.promise: Learn one geographic habit: move from what and where to why, then prove the answer through a map relationship.
lessons.sourceSummary: Adapted from the local Geography Foundation architecture, NCERT Geography as a Discipline, and the Day 1 India Map Intelligence relationship drills.

scenes.title: Ask the geographic question
scenes.objective: Start with what, where, and why before collecting facts.
scenes.narration: Geography studies spatial distribution. Identify what exists, locate where it appears, and then ask why it is here rather than somewhere else.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can ask what, where, and why for one real place instead of listing isolated facts.

scenes.title: Read location as a relationship
scenes.objective: Separate absolute location from relative location.
scenes.narration: Coordinates give absolute location. Relative location explains a place through its relationships with rivers, coasts, passes, markets, routes, and neighbouring regions.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why coordinates locate a place but relationships help explain its importance.

scenes.title: Use site, situation, and scale
scenes.objective: Connect a place's physical site with its wider situation.
scenes.narration: Site describes what a place is like. Situation describes where it sits in relation to other places. Scale changes the answer: a local pattern may look different at the regional or national level.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can distinguish site from situation and name one case where scale changes the answer.

scenes.title: Practice India map relationships
scenes.objective: Train the pair-matching habit used in UPSC map questions.
scenes.narration: Do not memorize names alone. Practice relationships: river to sea, pass to state, strait to water bodies, lake to type, and national park to location.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can give one correct India relationship and explain the common near-correct trap.

scenes.title: Explain why here, not there
scenes.objective: Compress the lesson into a short spoken answer.
scenes.narration: Explain one place through what it is, where it is, why the location matters, one relationship visible on a map, and one UPSC statement trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one map relationship to the AI teacher in their own words.

geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.label: Ganga -> Bay of Bengal
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.cue: Follow the river relationship, not only the river name.
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.proofPrompt: Map relationship: the Ganga drains into the Bay of Bengal. This is useful because UPSC often tests river-to-sea pairs. Trap: do not reverse the outlet or confuse a tributary with the main river.

geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.label: Shipki La -> Himachal Pradesh -> Sutlej enters India
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.cue: Connect the pass, its state, and the linked river fact.
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.proofPrompt: Map relationship: Shipki La is in Himachal Pradesh, and the Sutlej enters India through this pass. This is stronger than memorizing either name alone. Trap: do not confuse Shipki La with a Sikkim pass.

geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.label: Nathu La -> Sikkim -> India-Tibet trade route
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.cue: Attach a map location to its strategic role.
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.proofPrompt: Map relationship: Nathu La is in Sikkim and links India with Tibet through a trade route. The situation matters because border geography gives the location strategic value. Trap: do not assign Nathu La to Arunachal Pradesh.

geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.label: Chilika -> Odisha -> brackish lagoon
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.cue: Separate famous water bodies by type, not familiarity.
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.proofPrompt: Map relationship: Chilika is in Odisha and is a brackish lagoon. The type matters for UPSC statements. Trap: do not describe Chilika as a freshwater lake or confuse it with India's largest saline lake.

geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.label: Bab-el-Mandeb -> Red Sea and Gulf of Aden
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.cue: Name the two connected water bodies before marking the pair.
geographyDay1MapRelationshipDrills.proofPrompt: Map relationship: Bab-el-Mandeb connects the Red Sea with the Gulf of Aden. It is a strategic strait on the Suez route. Trap: do not say that it directly connects the Red Sea with the Mediterranean Sea.

lessons.title: Origin and Evolution of Earth
lessons.promise: Follow one causal chain from expansion of space to a layered Earth with an atmosphere and hydrosphere.
lessons.sourceSummary: Adapted from the local NCERT Origin and Evolution of Earth source and the portal's Universe-to-Earth animation blueprints.

scenes.title: Begin with expansion, not explosion
scenes.objective: Correct the first universe misconception before building the sequence.
scenes.narration: Start with a hot dense state. Space itself expands and the universe cools. Do not imagine a bomb exploding from one central point into pre-existing empty space.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why expansion of space is more accurate than explosion into empty space.

scenes.title: Let gravity organize matter
scenes.objective: Connect cooling with matter formation, stars, galaxies, and a solar nebula.
scenes.narration: Cooling allows matter to stabilize. Gravity amplifies uneven density, builds clouds and stars, and collapses a rotating solar nebula into a disk.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect cooling, gravity, clouds, stars, and the solar disk in the correct order.

scenes.title: Build planets through accretion
scenes.objective: Explain how the solar disk becomes planets and a young Earth.
scenes.narration: Most mass concentrates in the Sun. Remaining dust and rocky material collide and accrete. Inner rocky planets form in the hotter zone; outer planets retain more gas and ice.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can define accretion and explain why inner and outer planets differ.

scenes.title: Differentiate the young Earth
scenes.objective: Bridge Earth origin with the next lesson on Earth interior.
scenes.narration: Heating and density differences sort Earth internally. Heavier material sinks and lighter material rises. Cooling, outgassing, and condensation help form the atmosphere and hydrosphere.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain differentiation and one atmosphere-hydrosphere sequence without listing disconnected facts.

scenes.title: Speak the five-step chain
scenes.objective: Compress the lesson into an oral explanation for the AI teacher.
scenes.narration: Explain the causal chain: expansion, cooling and gravity, solar-disk accretion, Earth differentiation, then atmosphere and hydrosphere. End with the explosion misconception.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to reproduce the full chain and one UPSC trap in their own words.

geographyDay2UniverseStages.label: Space expands
geographyDay2UniverseStages.explanation: The early universe expands and cools. Treat the Big Bang as expansion of space, not an explosion into empty space.
geographyDay2UniverseStages.proof: Trap: do not imagine a central blast moving through pre-existing empty space.

geographyDay2UniverseStages.label: Gravity builds structure
geographyDay2UniverseStages.explanation: As the universe cools, matter stabilizes. Gravity amplifies density differences and organizes matter into clouds, stars, and galaxies.
geographyDay2UniverseStages.proof: Cause chain: cooling allows matter; gravity organizes uneven density.

geographyDay2UniverseStages.label: A solar disk forms
geographyDay2UniverseStages.explanation: A rotating cloud collapses into a disk. Most mass forms the Sun; remaining material collides and accretes into planets.
geographyDay2UniverseStages.proof: Trap: planets do not appear as finished bodies in one step.

geographyDay2UniverseStages.label: Young Earth separates
geographyDay2UniverseStages.explanation: Heating and density differences sort Earth internally: heavier material sinks, lighter material rises, and layers emerge.
geographyDay2UniverseStages.proof: Bridge: differentiation prepares the next lesson on Earth interior and plate movement.

geographyDay2UniverseStages.label: Earth becomes habitable
geographyDay2UniverseStages.explanation: Cooling, outgassing, condensation, and later biological change build the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and conditions for life.
geographyDay2UniverseStages.proof: Recall chain: expansion -> gravity -> accretion -> differentiation -> atmosphere and hydrosphere.

lessons.title: Interior of Earth and Plate Movement
lessons.promise: Move from seismic evidence to layers, mantle behavior, plate boundaries, and the map pattern of landforms and hazards.
lessons.sourceSummary: Adapted from the portal's Earth-interior-to-plate-movement animation blueprint and the compact 20-day Geography bridge.

scenes.title: Infer layers from waves
scenes.objective: Start with evidence before naming Earth layers.
scenes.narration: Earth interior is not directly visible. Use seismic behavior: P-waves bend as material changes, while S-waves stop at the liquid outer core.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why seismic waves are evidence for a layered Earth.

scenes.title: Connect layers with movement
scenes.objective: Separate structural layers from the moving plate system.
scenes.narration: Distinguish crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core. Then place rigid lithospheric plates over the weaker plastic asthenosphere and connect internal heat with mantle movement.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why the mantle is not a fully liquid magma ocean.

scenes.title: Compare the three boundaries
scenes.objective: Turn movement direction into map-visible outcomes.
scenes.narration: At divergent boundaries plates move apart and new crust forms. At convergent boundaries plates collide or subduct. At transform boundaries plates slide past each other and release stress.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can compare divergent, convergent, and transform motion with one landform or hazard cue each.

scenes.title: Control the plate traps
scenes.objective: Correct common textbook oversimplifications.
scenes.narration: Do not say every boundary creates volcanoes. Do not say plates float on a liquid mantle. Match the boundary setting with the correct landform and hazard pattern.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state one almost-correct plate statement and its exception.

scenes.title: Speak the five-step chain
scenes.objective: Compress the visual into an oral explanation for the AI teacher.
scenes.narration: Explain the chain: seismic evidence, layered Earth, mantle behavior, plate movement, then clustered landforms and hazards. End with one boundary-specific trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain the evidence-to-hazard chain in their own words.

geographyDay3PlateStages.label: Read seismic evidence
geographyDay3PlateStages.explanation: P-waves bend as material changes. S-waves stop at the liquid outer core. Earth layers are inferred from evidence, not direct observation.
geographyDay3PlateStages.proof: Trap: S-waves cannot pass through liquids; P-waves can pass through solids and liquids.

geographyDay3PlateStages.label: Separate the layers
geographyDay3PlateStages.explanation: Crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core describe internal structure. Rigid lithosphere moves over the weaker plastic asthenosphere.
geographyDay3PlateStages.proof: Trap: the mantle is not a fully liquid ocean of magma.

geographyDay3PlateStages.label: Follow internal heat
geographyDay3PlateStages.explanation: Internal heat and mantle behavior create convection-style movement. This gives the plate system a physical driver.
geographyDay3PlateStages.proof: Cause chain: internal heat -> mantle movement -> lithospheric plate movement.

geographyDay3PlateStages.label: Compare boundaries
geographyDay3PlateStages.explanation: Divergent boundaries move apart, convergent boundaries collide or subduct, and transform boundaries slide past each other.
geographyDay3PlateStages.proof: Map cue: ridge, trench or mountain, and earthquake fault reveal different boundary settings.

geographyDay3PlateStages.label: Predict the pattern
geographyDay3PlateStages.explanation: Earthquakes, volcanoes, ridges, trenches, and mountains cluster because plate-boundary processes are spatially organized.
geographyDay3PlateStages.proof: Recall chain: seismic evidence -> layers -> mantle behavior -> boundaries -> landforms and hazards.

scenes.objective: Compress the visual into an oral explanation for the AI teacher.
scenes.narration: Explain the chain: seismic evidence, layered Earth, mantle behavior, plate movement, then clustered landforms and hazards. End with one boundary-specific trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain the evidence-to-hazard chain in their own words.

lessons.title: Geomorphic Processes
lessons.promise: Explain how exposed rock becomes weathered material, transported sediment, depositional landforms, and slope-failure risk.
lessons.sourceSummary: Adapted from the portal's weathering-erosion-mass-wasting animation blueprint and the compact 20-day Geography foundation.

scenes.title: Expose relief to external forces
scenes.objective: Place geomorphic processes after uplift and before landforms.
scenes.narration: Internal forces create relief. Once rock is exposed, sunlight, rainfall, temperature change, organisms, and gravity begin reshaping the surface.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can distinguish relief creation from later denudation.

scenes.title: Separate weathering from erosion
scenes.objective: Build the core process distinction before examples.
scenes.narration: Weathering breaks rock in place through physical, chemical, or biological action. Erosion begins when an agent removes and transports loosened material.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain weathering in place and erosion as removal plus transport.

scenes.title: Follow sediment to deposition
scenes.objective: Connect agent energy with depositional landforms.
scenes.narration: Water, wind, ice, and waves move sediment. When energy falls, material settles and builds a fan, floodplain, beach, or another depositional form.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one agent with erosion, transport, and deposition.

scenes.title: Add gravity and slope risk
scenes.objective: Connect denudation with landslide logic.
scenes.narration: Mass wasting is downslope movement under gravity. Water saturation, steep slope, shaking, or undercutting can trigger sudden movement.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate gravity-driven mass wasting from erosion by a transporting agent.

scenes.title: Speak the process chain
scenes.objective: Compress the sequence for AI-teacher discussion.
scenes.narration: Explain the chain: uplift exposes rock, weathering breaks it in place, erosion removes and transports it, deposition settles it, and gravity can fail slopes suddenly.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain the denudation sequence and one UPSC vocabulary trap.

geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.label: Expose the rock
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.explanation: Uplifted rock enters the external-process system when sunlight, rainfall, temperature change, organisms, and gravity begin acting on it.
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.proof: Start with exposed relief: exogenic processes reshape land after uplift creates it.

geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.label: Break rock in place
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.explanation: Physical, chemical, and biological weathering weaken rock where it stands. Climate and organisms change which process dominates.
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.proof: Trap: weathering is breakdown in place; it does not require removal.

geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.label: Remove and transport
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.explanation: Water, wind, ice, and waves remove loosened material and transport it. The moving agent matters for the resulting landform.
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.proof: Cause chain: weathering supplies material; erosion removes and transports it.

geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.label: Deposit when energy falls
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.explanation: When a transporting agent loses energy, sediment settles and creates depositional landforms such as fans, floodplains, or beaches.
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.proof: Trap: deposition is not breakdown; it is settling after transport loses energy.

geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.label: Read slope failure
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.explanation: Gravity moves material downslope. Water saturation, slope angle, earthquake shaking, or undercutting can trigger landslides and other mass movements.
geographyDay4GeomorphicStages.proof: Recall chain: exposure -> weathering -> erosion and transport -> deposition; gravity can move slopes suddenly.

lessons.title: Climatology Base
lessons.promise: Derive global winds from unequal heating, pressure contrast, circulation, Earth's rotation, and pressure-belt organization.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Day 5 class pack and Monsoon Simulator preparation contract into a compact portal-native learner visual.

scenes.title: Begin with uneven solar energy
scenes.objective: Derive atmospheric motion from unequal heating instead of memorizing wind names.
scenes.narration: Direct rays concentrate solar energy near low latitudes while slanting rays spread energy across a wider surface toward the poles.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why the atmosphere begins with an energy imbalance.

scenes.title: Build pressure and circulation
scenes.objective: Connect heating, density, pressure gradient, rising air, and sinking air.
scenes.narration: Warm air expands and rises while cooler denser air sinks. Pressure differences move air from high pressure toward low pressure and redistribute heat.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect unequal heating with pressure-gradient force and circulation.

scenes.title: Add rotation and wind belts
scenes.objective: Place Coriolis deflection inside the global circulation map.
scenes.narration: Earth's rotation deflects moving air right in the Northern Hemisphere and left in the Southern Hemisphere. Pressure belts then organize trade winds, westerlies, and polar easterlies.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state hemisphere-wise Coriolis direction and name the major wind belts.

scenes.title: Correct the common UPSC reversal
scenes.objective: Separate the force that starts motion from the effect that curves motion.
scenes.narration: Pressure gradient starts wind. Coriolis deflects moving air but does not create the original pressure contrast. Belts also shift seasonally with apparent solar movement.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject a statement that treats Coriolis as the original cause of wind.

scenes.title: Speak the circulation chain
scenes.objective: Compress climatology base for AI-teacher discussion.
scenes.narration: Explain the chain: unequal heating creates pressure contrast, pressure gradient moves air, rotation deflects it, and global belts organize planetary winds before seasonal monsoon shifts.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain the circulation chain and one direction trap.

geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.label: Read unequal heating
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.explanation: Direct rays concentrate energy near low latitudes while slanting rays spread the same energy across a wider surface toward the poles.
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.proof: Start with energy imbalance: latitude and sunlight angle create unequal heating.

geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.label: Create pressure contrast
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.explanation: Warmer air expands and rises, creating a low-pressure tendency. Cooler denser air sinks, creating a high-pressure tendency.
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.proof: Cause chain: unequal heating creates pressure differences; pressure gradient begins air movement.

geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.label: Move air high to low
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.explanation: Air moves from high pressure toward low pressure. Rising and sinking branches redistribute heat and build large circulation cells.
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.proof: Trap: pressure gradient starts wind, but the final path is shaped by a rotating Earth.

geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.label: Deflect moving air
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.explanation: Earth's rotation deflects moving air to the right in the Northern Hemisphere and to the left in the Southern Hemisphere.
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.proof: UPSC trap: Coriolis changes direction; it does not create the original pressure-gradient force.

geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.label: Recall global wind belts
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.explanation: Pressure belts and circulation cells organize trade winds, westerlies, and polar easterlies. Their seasonal shift prepares monsoon logic.
geographyDay5ClimatologyStages.proof: Recall chain: unequal heating -> pressure gradient -> circulation -> Coriolis deflection -> planetary wind belts.

lessons.title: Ocean System
lessons.promise: Connect ocean relief, temperature, salinity, circulation, coastal climate, fog, deserts, upwelling, fisheries, and current-location pairs.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Day 6 class pack into a compact portal-native learner visual for ocean-current map logic.

scenes.title: Begin with basin relief
scenes.objective: Place the ocean floor beneath current and climate logic.
scenes.narration: Continental shelf, slope, abyssal plain, ridge, and trench shape the ocean basin. Shelf seas also matter for fisheries and coastal activity.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can trace the coast-to-deep-ocean relief sequence.

scenes.title: Add water properties and circulation
scenes.objective: Connect temperature, salinity, density, winds, rotation, and basin shape.
scenes.narration: Latitude, evaporation, precipitation, rivers, ice, and mixing change temperature and salinity. Winds, rotation, basin shape, and density differences organize circulation.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why currents cannot be reduced to one variable.

scenes.title: Map warm and cold current effects
scenes.objective: Attach current type to location-specific climate and fishery outcomes.
scenes.narration: Warm currents usually add heat and moisture. Cold currents can support coastal deserts, fog, and nutrient-rich upwelling. Use a current-location pair before stating an effect.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one warm-current pair and one cold-current pair.

scenes.title: Correct the fisheries shortcut
scenes.objective: Separate salinity facts from upwelling and nutrient logic.
scenes.narration: Do not explain productive fisheries through salinity alone. Upwelling brings nutrient-rich deeper water toward the surface and supports biological productivity.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject a statement that treats salinity alone as the cause of fisheries.

scenes.title: Speak the ocean-system chain
scenes.objective: Compress the map logic for AI-teacher discussion.
scenes.narration: Explain the chain: relief shapes the basin, temperature and salinity influence density, circulation redistributes heat, and current-location pairs explain climate, fog, deserts, upwelling, and fisheries.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one current-location pair and one UPSC trap.

geographyDay6OceanStages.label: Read the ocean floor
geographyDay6OceanStages.explanation: Continental shelf, slope, abyssal plain, ridge, and trench shape the ocean basin before water properties and currents are added.
geographyDay6OceanStages.proof: Start with basin structure: shelf and slope connect the coast to deep-ocean relief.

geographyDay6OceanStages.label: Compare water properties
geographyDay6OceanStages.explanation: Latitude, evaporation, precipitation, river inflow, ice, and mixing change temperature and salinity. Together they influence seawater density.
geographyDay6OceanStages.proof: Cause chain: temperature and salinity alter density, but neither variable should be read in isolation.

geographyDay6OceanStages.label: Trace warm and cold currents
geographyDay6OceanStages.explanation: Winds, rotation, basin shape, and density differences organize surface and deep-ocean circulation. Warm and cold currents move heat across the map.
geographyDay6OceanStages.proof: Trap: a current name is useful only when its location, direction, and temperature effect are connected.

geographyDay6OceanStages.label: Connect coastal effects
geographyDay6OceanStages.explanation: Warm currents often add heat and moisture. Cold currents can support coastal deserts, fog, and nutrient-rich upwelling that improves fisheries.
geographyDay6OceanStages.proof: UPSC trap: salinity alone does not explain fisheries; upwelling and nutrient supply matter.

geographyDay6OceanStages.label: Recall current-location pairs
geographyDay6OceanStages.explanation: Use one pair at a time: Gulf Stream and North Atlantic Drift warm western Europe, while the Peru Current supports upwelling and coastal aridity.
geographyDay6OceanStages.proof: Recall chain: relief -> properties -> circulation -> coastal effects -> current-location map pair.

lessons.title: Physical Geography Consolidation
lessons.promise: Connect location, Earth structure, tectonics, landforms, climatology, and oceanography into one UPSC-ready physical-geography framework.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Week 1 consolidation pack into a compact portal-native integration visual.

scenes.title: Begin with map language
scenes.objective: Use location and scale as the entry point for integrated physical geography.
scenes.narration: Coordinates and relative location tell you where a pattern sits. Scale and map relationships keep later physical explanations spatially precise.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can attach one physical-geography process to a location or map relationship.

scenes.title: Connect internal driver and surface change
scenes.objective: Link tectonic relief creation with geomorphic modification.
scenes.narration: Earth's layered interior and plate movement create relief. Weathering, erosion, transport, deposition, and mass wasting reshape that relief after exposure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate relief creation from relief modification.

scenes.title: Add atmospheric and ocean circulation
scenes.objective: Connect map patterns with moving heat and moisture.
scenes.narration: Unequal heating creates pressure gradients and winds. Ocean currents redistribute heat and connect relief and circulation with regional rainfall, fog, deserts, and fisheries.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can add one climate or ocean effect to a physical map explanation.

scenes.title: Reject isolated memorization
scenes.objective: Prepare for mixed UPSC statements that join multiple systems.
scenes.narration: Integrated questions often combine two systems. Reject statements that reverse cause and effect, mix location pairs, or treat one variable as the complete explanation.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify one near-correct mixed-system statement and its hidden exception.

scenes.title: Speak one integrated chain
scenes.objective: Generate the first weak-topic list through AI-teacher discussion.
scenes.narration: Explain one chain: locate the pattern, name the internal or external driver, trace the process, map the climate or ocean effect, and reject one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one integrated chain and identify the first weak topic.

geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.label: Anchor the map
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.explanation: Coordinates, relative location, scale, and map relationships tell you where a process occurs before you explain why it occurs.
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.proof: Start with place: every physical-geography answer needs a location or map relationship.

geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.label: Add the internal driver
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.explanation: Earth's layered interior and plate movement create relief, ridges, trenches, mountains, volcanoes, earthquakes, and major hazard zones.
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.proof: Cause chain: internal heat and plate movement create the relief framework.

geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.label: Reshape relief
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.explanation: Weathering, erosion, transport, deposition, and mass wasting modify tectonic relief after it reaches the surface.
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.proof: Trap: tectonics creates relief; weathering and erosion reshape it.

geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.label: Move heat and moisture
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.explanation: Unequal heating, pressure belts, winds, and ocean currents redistribute heat and moisture and explain regional climate effects.
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.proof: Map chain: pressure belts and currents connect physical mechanisms with regional outcomes.

geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.label: Answer as one system
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.explanation: A strong UPSC explanation links place, internal driver, surface process, climate or ocean effect, and one near-correct statement trap.
geographyDay7ConsolidationStages.proof: Recall chain: locate -> explain the driver -> trace the process -> map the effect -> reject the trap.

lessons.title: India Physiography
lessons.promise: Read India relief as the base layer behind rivers, monsoon, soils, agriculture, resources, settlement, and disaster risk.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged India Map Command pack into a compact portal-native relief map visual.

scenes.title: Begin with the India relief frame
scenes.objective: Use relief zones as the organizing map layer for Indian geography.
scenes.narration: India physiography becomes easier when you read location, height, slope, and coastline as one base layer instead of memorizing disconnected regions.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state why relief is the foundation for later river, monsoon, soil, agriculture, resource, and hazard questions.

scenes.title: Contrast young mountains, plains, and plateau
scenes.objective: Separate tectonic, depositional, and ancient-block relief logic.
scenes.narration: The young Himalayas influence drainage, climate, and hazards. Alluvial deposition builds the northern plains. The older peninsular block organizes mineral belts, escarpments, and river slope.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can contrast the Himalayas, northern plains, and peninsular plateau through one process each.

scenes.title: Add desert, coasts, and islands
scenes.objective: Complete the India relief map with edge systems.
scenes.narration: Place the Thar desert in the northwest, compare the narrow western and broader eastern coastal plains, and attach island groups to their distinct settings.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can locate one desert, coast, or island relationship and explain why it matters.

scenes.title: Reject mixed-location statements
scenes.objective: Prepare for state, boundary, relief, climate, and river pair-matching traps.
scenes.narration: UPSC often mixes a correct physiographic idea with the wrong state, boundary, river behavior, or climate consequence. Separate the relief zone from its effect before accepting the pair.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify one near-correct India physiography statement and its hidden location error.

scenes.title: Explain one relief-to-risk chain
scenes.objective: Move into AI-teacher discussion with one map-backed explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one relief division. State its map location, explain the process behind it, connect one river or climate effect, add one economic use or risk, and reject one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one India relief division through map, mechanism, consequence, and trap.

geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.label: Read the relief frame
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.explanation: Treat India physiography as one connected relief framework. Location, height, slope, and coastline shape the later river, climate, soil, agriculture, resource, and hazard patterns.
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.proof: Start with the base layer: relief controls the map logic behind many Indian geography questions.

geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.label: Anchor the Himalayas
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.explanation: The young fold mountains form a high northern arc. They influence river origin, monsoon uplift, snow and glacier storage, passes, earthquakes, and landslide exposure.
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.proof: Trap: the Himalayas are not only a boundary; they also shape drainage, climate, and hazard risk.

geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.label: Trace the northern plains
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.explanation: The Indus-Ganga-Brahmaputra plains are built by alluvium. Low relief, fertile soils, dense settlement, irrigation potential, shifting channels, and flood risk belong to the same depositional story.
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.proof: Map chain: sediment deposition creates fertile plains while also preserving flood vulnerability.

geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.label: Read the plateau core
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.explanation: The plateau is an old, stable block with mineral belts, dissected uplands, escarpments, and a general eastward river slope. Its hard-rock structure differs sharply from the young alluvial plains.
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.proof: Trap: plateau rivers and resources follow slope and geology; they do not copy Himalayan river behavior.

geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.label: Connect desert, coasts, and islands
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.explanation: The Thar desert, western and eastern coastal plains, and island groups complete the relief map. Each edge changes rainfall, ports, deltas, coral or volcanic settings, and disaster exposure.
geographyDay8IndiaReliefStages.proof: Recall chain: locate relief zone -> explain process -> connect one use or risk -> reject the mixed-location trap.

lessons.title: Indian Drainage
lessons.promise: Explain Indian river behavior through origin, slope, basin, tributaries, state path, outlet, and one UPSC map trap.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged India Map Command drainage pack into a compact portal-native river-system visual.

scenes.title: Begin with the drainage method
scenes.objective: Use one repeatable map sequence instead of memorizing river lists.
scenes.narration: Trace every river through source, relief, slope, basin, tributaries, state path, outlet, and consequence. The same frame works for Himalayan and peninsular systems.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state the source-to-outlet sequence for reading an Indian river system.

scenes.title: Contrast Himalayan and peninsular behavior
scenes.objective: Separate snow-rain-fed young systems from older plateau-controlled systems.
scenes.narration: Himalayan systems are largely perennial and sediment-rich. Many peninsular systems depend more strongly on rainfall and plateau slope, so their seasonal behavior and outlets differ.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why Himalayan and peninsular rivers do not behave identically.

scenes.title: Attach basin, tributary, and outlet
scenes.objective: Convert river names into spatial relationships.
scenes.narration: Add one basin, one tributary, the states crossed, and the outlet. Connect the map with a delta, estuary, floodplain, irrigation, or erosion consequence.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can trace one Indian river system through a basin relationship and its outlet.

scenes.title: Reject swapped pairs
scenes.objective: Prepare for tributary, source-state, direction, delta, and estuary traps.
scenes.narration: When a tributary pair appears, face downstream before deciding left or right bank. When an outlet pair appears, trace flow direction before deciding delta or estuary.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct river or tributary pair by tracing direction.

scenes.title: Explain one river end to end
scenes.objective: Move into AI-teacher discussion with one map-backed river explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one river system. State its source, slope, basin, tributary relationship, state path, outlet, one use or risk, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one river system from source to outlet and reject one swapped pair.

geographyDay9DrainageStages.label: Build the drainage frame
geographyDay9DrainageStages.explanation: Read every river through source, relief, slope, basin, tributaries, state path, and outlet. This turns river lists into one repeatable map method.
geographyDay9DrainageStages.proof: Start with the method: source -> slope -> basin -> tributary -> outlet -> consequence.

geographyDay9DrainageStages.label: Trace Himalayan rivers
geographyDay9DrainageStages.explanation: Indus, Ganga, and Brahmaputra systems are largely snow-rain fed, perennial, sediment-rich, and linked with broad plains, shifting channels, erosion, floods, and deposition.
geographyDay9DrainageStages.proof: Map chain: young relief and high sediment load help explain plains, floods, and changing channels.

geographyDay9DrainageStages.label: Compare plateau rivers
geographyDay9DrainageStages.explanation: Many peninsular rivers depend more strongly on rainfall and plateau slope. East-flowing systems commonly build deltas, while important west-flowing systems use rift-valley routes and estuaries.
geographyDay9DrainageStages.proof: Trap: do not apply one east-flowing-delta rule mechanically to every peninsular river.

geographyDay9DrainageStages.label: Connect outlets and effects
geographyDay9DrainageStages.explanation: Drainage behavior shapes irrigation, groundwater recharge, erosion, flood risk, delta agriculture, estuaries, wetlands, and settlement. Outlet logic converts a map line into a real geography answer.
geographyDay9DrainageStages.proof: Answer chain: river behavior -> outlet pattern -> one use or risk -> one regional example.

geographyDay9DrainageStages.label: Reject tributary traps
geographyDay9DrainageStages.explanation: Pair-matching questions often swap source states, left-bank and right-bank tributaries, east-flowing and west-flowing rivers, deltas, estuaries, or outlets. Trace direction before judging the pair.
geographyDay9DrainageStages.proof: Recall chain: locate source -> face downstream -> place tributary -> trace outlet -> reject the swapped pair.

lessons.title: Indian Monsoon
lessons.promise: Explain monsoon as a moving pressure-wind-rainfall system with regional contrasts, seasonal phases, and uncertainty.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged India Map Command pack and Monsoon Simulator route into a compact portal-native sequence visual.

scenes.title: Begin with seasonal pressure contrast
scenes.objective: Build the monsoon from land-sea heating instead of memorizing rainfall dates.
scenes.narration: Stronger summer heating over the subcontinent helps organize lower pressure relative to the surrounding ocean. This contrast prepares moisture-bearing inflow.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state why seasonal land-sea thermal contrast matters for monsoon circulation.

scenes.title: Shift the ITCZ and bend the flow
scenes.objective: Connect the seasonal rain-belt shift with southwest monsoon circulation.
scenes.narration: The ITCZ shifts northward. Cross-equatorial flow bends under rotation and carries moisture toward India as part of the southwest monsoon system.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect the ITCZ shift, cross-equatorial flow, and southwest monsoon direction.

scenes.title: Split branches and explain rainfall contrasts
scenes.objective: Use relief and branch paths to map uneven rainfall.
scenes.narration: Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal branches meet different relief barriers. Orographic uplift, rain shadows, and regional pathways produce uneven rainfall.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one branch and one regional rainfall contrast through relief.

scenes.title: Reject static and one-factor explanations
scenes.objective: Prepare for phase, direction, region, and variability traps.
scenes.narration: Read onset, active spells, breaks, and retreat as a moving sequence. Treat ENSO, IOD, jet streams, and local factors as influences, not complete one-factor explanations.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one direction, rainfall-region, or single-factor monsoon statement.

scenes.title: Explain the monsoon in five steps
scenes.objective: Move into AI-teacher discussion with one regional contrast and one uncertainty signal.
scenes.narration: Explain thermal contrast, ITCZ movement, southwest flow, one branch and rainfall contrast, active-break-retreat rhythm, and one variability factor with its limitation.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain monsoon as a moving system and reject one oversimplified statement.

geographyDay10MonsoonStages.label: Create thermal contrast
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.explanation: Summer heating strengthens low pressure over the subcontinent while the surrounding ocean is relatively cooler. This pressure contrast prepares moisture-bearing inflow.
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.proof: Start with the driver: stronger land heating helps organize a seasonal pressure contrast.

geographyDay10MonsoonStages.label: Shift the rain belt
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.explanation: The rain belt shifts northward with seasonal heating. Cross-equatorial flow bends under rotation and feeds the southwest monsoon circulation.
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.proof: Map chain: seasonal heating -> ITCZ shift -> cross-equatorial flow -> southwest monsoon.

geographyDay10MonsoonStages.label: Split the moisture branches
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.explanation: Arabian Sea and Bay of Bengal branches meet different relief barriers. Orographic uplift, rain shadows, and regional pathways explain strongly uneven rainfall.
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.proof: Trap: monsoon arrival does not mean uniform rainfall across India.

geographyDay10MonsoonStages.label: Read onset, break, and retreat
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.explanation: The monsoon is not one static event. Onset, active spells, breaks, and retreat shift the location and intensity of rainfall through the season.
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.proof: Recall sequence: onset -> active phase -> break -> retreat.

geographyDay10MonsoonStages.label: Add variability signals
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.explanation: Jet streams, western disturbances, ENSO, IOD, sea-surface conditions, relief, and local factors modify the seasonal pattern. No single signal explains every year.
geographyDay10MonsoonStages.proof: UPSC trap: one variability factor can influence rainfall without becoming a complete one-factor explanation.

lessons.title: Climate Regions of India
lessons.promise: Explain why nearby Indian regions can receive sharply different rainfall and temperature patterns through relief, winds, altitude, latitude, distance from sea, and disturbances.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged India Map Command climate pack into a compact portal-native regional-comparison visual.

scenes.title: Begin with regional climate controls
scenes.objective: Organize Indian climate through interacting controls instead of isolated facts.
scenes.narration: Latitude, altitude, relief, distance from sea, pressure systems, and winds work together. Start with the control framework before memorizing regional totals.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can name the main controls that create regional climate variation in India.

scenes.title: Trace moisture and relief
scenes.objective: Connect monsoon pathways with windward rain and leeward rain shadow.
scenes.narration: Moisture-bearing winds meet relief barriers. Orographic uplift concentrates rain on windward slopes while descending air leaves leeward regions drier.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why nearby windward and leeward places may receive very different rainfall.

scenes.title: Add winter precipitation and temperature range
scenes.objective: Compare seasonal pathways and maritime versus continental effects.
scenes.narration: Western disturbances bring winter precipitation to northwest India and Himalayan snow. Coastal influence moderates temperature range, while interior, desert, plateau, and mountain settings differ.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one winter precipitation region and one temperature-range contrast with its controlling factor.

scenes.title: Reject regional mismatches
scenes.objective: Prepare for rainfall amount, season, wind-source, and location traps.
scenes.narration: A climate statement can use a correct mechanism with the wrong region or season. Separate rainfall source, relief relationship, temperature control, and map example before accepting a pair.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct climate-region statement and identify the mismatch.

scenes.title: Explain one climate region
scenes.objective: Move into AI-teacher discussion with one map-backed regional comparison.
scenes.narration: Choose one Indian climate region. Name its controls, rainfall season, temperature pattern, one map example, and one trap involving a disturbance, rain shadow, or maritime effect.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one region and compare it with a nearby contrasting region.

geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.label: Read the climate controls
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.explanation: Latitude, altitude, relief, distance from sea, pressure systems, and winds work together. Start with controls before memorizing rainfall totals or temperature values.
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.proof: Method: locate the region -> identify controls -> explain rainfall and temperature -> reject the mismatch.

geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.label: Trace rainfall corridors
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.explanation: Moisture branches meet relief barriers and lose rain unevenly. Windward slopes, the northeast pathway, and the Himalayan barrier create strong regional contrasts.
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.proof: Map chain: moisture path + relief barrier + uplift explain rainfall concentration.

geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.label: Explain nearby contrasts
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.explanation: A mountain barrier can produce a wet windward side and a drier leeward side within a short distance. Nearby places can therefore carry sharply different climate signatures.
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.proof: Trap: proximity alone does not guarantee similar rainfall when relief changes the wind path.

geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.label: Add winter disturbance
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.explanation: Western disturbances bring winter precipitation to northwest India and snow to Himalayan regions. They belong to a different seasonal pathway from the southwest monsoon.
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.proof: UPSC trap: western disturbances are not the source of India's southwest monsoon rainfall.

geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.label: Compare climate regions
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.explanation: Coastal, interior, desert, plateau, mountain, and rain-shadow regions differ because their controls differ. Compare one map location, rainfall season, temperature range, and hidden exception.
geographyDay11ClimateRegionsStages.proof: Recall chain: control factors -> rainfall season -> temperature range -> map example -> regional trap.

lessons.title: Soils and Vegetation
lessons.promise: Connect soil and vegetation distribution with parent material, climate, relief, drainage, crops, degradation, and conservation.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged India Map Command pack into a compact portal-native geography-to-environment bridge visual.

scenes.title: Begin with soil-formation factors
scenes.objective: Build soil types from formation logic instead of memorized color lists.
scenes.narration: Parent material, climate, relief, drainage, organisms, and time work together. Soil properties emerge from that combined setting.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can name the main soil-formation factors and connect one factor with one property.

scenes.title: Map soil families and vegetation response
scenes.objective: Connect regional soil distribution with crop and forest patterns.
scenes.narration: Map major soil families through region, property, crop advantage, and limitation. Then add vegetation as a response to rainfall, temperature, altitude, soil, and human pressure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one soil family and one vegetation type through their physical setting.

scenes.title: Add degradation and conservation
scenes.objective: Bridge physical geography with environment questions.
scenes.narration: Soil erosion, salinity, desertification, forest degradation, overuse, and drainage stress alter regional outcomes. Attach one conservation response to one pressure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one regional degradation pressure with a practical conservation response.

scenes.title: Reject cross-matched pairs
scenes.objective: Prepare for soil-color, crop, rainfall-zone, forest, and state-example traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may use a correct soil or forest feature with the wrong crop, state, rainfall zone, or limitation. Check the complete relationship before accepting the pair.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct soil-location-crop or vegetation-region pair.

scenes.title: Explain one soil or forest relationship
scenes.objective: Move into AI-teacher discussion with one applied geography-environment chain.
scenes.narration: Choose one soil or vegetation type. State the formation or control factor, region, crop or species link, limitation, conservation issue, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one geography-environment chain and reject one swapped pair.

geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.label: Build soil from factors
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.explanation: Parent material, climate, relief, drainage, organisms, and time work together. Start with formation factors before memorizing soil-color and crop pairs.
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.proof: Method: formation factors -> soil property -> regional distribution -> crop or forest link -> limitation.

geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.label: Map major soil families
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.explanation: Alluvial, black, red-yellow, laterite, desert, mountain, and saline soils carry different regional patterns, properties, crop advantages, and limitations.
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.proof: Trap: one soil name is incomplete without region, property, crop link, and limitation.

geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.label: Connect vegetation response
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.explanation: Rainfall, temperature, altitude, soil, and human pressure influence forest distribution. Vegetation is not a separate list; it responds to the physical setting.
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.proof: Map chain: rainfall + temperature + altitude + soil shape vegetation pattern.

geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.label: Add degradation pressure
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.explanation: Erosion, salinity, desertification, forest degradation, overuse, and drainage stress connect geography with environment and conservation questions.
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.proof: UPSC trap: productivity and degradation can coexist in the same region under different management conditions.

geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.label: Match use with conservation
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.explanation: A strong answer links one formation factor, region, soil or vegetation type, crop or species association, limitation, and practical conservation response.
geographyDay12SoilsVegetationStages.proof: Recall chain: factor -> region -> soil or forest -> use -> limitation -> conservation response.

lessons.title: Resources and Agriculture
lessons.promise: Explain resource and crop belts through geology, relief, water, climate, transport, market access, policy, and sustainability pressure.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged India Map Command pack into a compact portal-native resource-and-crop location visual.

scenes.title: Begin with location factors
scenes.objective: Explain why resources and crops cluster before naming belts.
scenes.narration: Start with geology, relief, soil, water, climate, transport, market access, and policy. These variables create resource and agricultural patterns.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can name the main location factors behind one resource or crop belt.

scenes.title: Trace resource and crop belts
scenes.objective: Connect geology-led resources with agro-climatic crop suitability.
scenes.narration: Mineral belts follow geology, while crop belts respond to soil, rainfall, temperature, water, market access, and technology. Keep each chain complete.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one mineral or energy belt and one crop belt through location logic.

scenes.title: Add water and sustainability pressure
scenes.objective: Connect productivity gains with long-term regional constraints.
scenes.narration: Irrigation and groundwater can raise output while creating depletion, salinity, waterlogging, energy stress, and unequal access. Attach one pressure to one region.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one productivity gain with one sustainability pressure.

scenes.title: Reject swapped belts
scenes.objective: Prepare for crop-region, mineral-state, energy-zone, and irrigation-impact traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may use a correct crop, mineral, state, or irrigation effect inside the wrong belt. Check the full location chain before accepting the pair.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct resource-region or crop-condition pair.

scenes.title: Explain one regional cluster
scenes.objective: Move into AI-teacher discussion with one resource-or-crop chain.
scenes.narration: Choose one crop, mineral, or energy resource. State the location factor, region, economic use, sustainability pressure, practical response, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one regional cluster and reject one swapped pair.

geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.label: Begin with location logic
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.explanation: Resources and crops do not appear randomly. Start with geology, relief, soil, water, climate, transport, market access, and policy before memorizing belts.
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.proof: Method: location factor -> regional belt -> economic use -> constraint -> sustainability issue.

geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.label: Trace resource belts
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.explanation: Mineral belts follow geology. Energy geography adds extraction site, transport route, demand center, environmental cost, and the limits of one-state shortcuts.
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.proof: Trap: a mineral-state pair is incomplete without geology, belt continuity, and economic use.

geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.label: Build crop suitability
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.explanation: Crop belts emerge from soil, rainfall, temperature, growing season, irrigation, market access, and technology. Suitability is a chain, not a single-factor fact.
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.proof: Map chain: soil + rain + temperature + water + market shape the crop belt.

geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.label: Add irrigation pressure
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.explanation: Irrigation and groundwater can increase output while also creating depletion, salinity, waterlogging, energy stress, and regional inequality.
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.proof: UPSC trap: higher irrigation can raise productivity and still produce long-term water stress.

geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.label: Explain one cluster
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.explanation: A strong answer links one crop, mineral, or energy resource with its location factor, region, economic use, pressure, and one near-correct UPSC trap.
geographyDay13ResourcesAgricultureStages.proof: Recall chain: factor -> belt -> use -> pressure -> response -> swapped-pair trap.

lessons.title: India Map Drill
lessons.promise: Convert two weeks of Indian geography into layered blank-map recall, integrated cause chains, weak-location repair, and mixed MCQ readiness.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Week 2 India Map Command consolidation pack into a compact portal-native layered map drill.

scenes.title: Begin with relief
scenes.objective: Build the India blank-map base before adding facts.
scenes.narration: Mark the Himalayas, plains, plateau, desert, coasts, and islands first. Relief explains the later drainage, climate, soil, resource, crop, and risk layers.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state why relief is the first blank-map layer.

scenes.title: Layer drainage and climate
scenes.objective: Connect slope and river systems with rainfall and regional climate.
scenes.narration: Trace drainage through source, slope, basin, direction, and outlet. Then overlay monsoon branches, uplift, rain shadow, western disturbances, and coastal or interior contrasts.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one relief-river-climate relationship.

scenes.title: Add productive geography
scenes.objective: Attach soils, vegetation, resources, agriculture, and sustainability pressure.
scenes.narration: Add soil and vegetation patterns, mineral and energy belts, crop suitability, irrigation gains, and one pressure such as depletion, salinity, erosion, or degradation.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can build one physical-base-to-productive-use chain.

scenes.title: Reject mixed-region traps
scenes.objective: Prepare for false statements made from individually correct facts.
scenes.narration: UPSC can combine a correct river, rainfall feature, soil, crop, mineral, or state example from different regions into one false statement. Verify the complete map chain.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one statement that mixes correct facts from different regions.

scenes.title: Generate five repair cards
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with an honest weak-location list.
scenes.narration: Choose the weakest five locations. For each one, state the map cue, cause, linked layer, and one near-correct trap before entering mixed MCQs.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one integrated India map chain and name the first weak location.

geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.label: Lay down relief
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.explanation: Begin every India map drill with the Himalayas, plains, plateau, desert, coasts, and islands. Relief is the base layer behind drainage, rainfall, soils, resources, settlement, and risk.
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.proof: Start with relief: it is the map layer that explains the later layers.

geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.label: Trace river systems
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.explanation: Add Himalayan and peninsular drainage through source, slope, basin, direction, tributaries, and outlet. Rivers make the relief layer operational.
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.proof: Map chain: relief -> slope -> river path -> basin -> outlet -> use or risk.

geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.label: Add climate regions
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.explanation: Overlay monsoon branches, windward uplift, rain shadow, western disturbances, coastal moderation, interior range, and climate-region contrasts.
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.proof: Trap: the same rainfall mechanism cannot be copied into every region or season.

geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.label: Connect productive belts
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.explanation: Layer soil families, vegetation response, mineral belts, energy zones, crop suitability, irrigation gains, and sustainability pressure onto the physical base.
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.proof: Integrated recall: physical base -> productive use -> constraint -> environment link.

geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.label: Repair weak locations
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.explanation: Close by tagging the weakest five locations. Each repair card needs a map cue, cause, linked layer, and one almost-correct statement trap.
geographyDay14IndiaMapDrillStages.proof: Recall chain: locate -> layer -> cause -> linked effect -> reject the mixed-region trap.

lessons.title: Population Geography
lessons.promise: Explain population patterns through density, distribution, physical base, economy, services, migration, demographic transition, and indicator discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native population-pattern visual.

scenes.title: Separate population indicators
scenes.objective: Distinguish density, distribution, growth, fertility, and migration before using data.
scenes.narration: Density is a ratio and distribution is a spatial pattern. Growth rate, fertility, age structure, and migration add different dimensions of population change.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can distinguish density from distribution and name one additional indicator.

scenes.title: Explain concentration
scenes.objective: Connect population patterns with physical and economic factors.
scenes.narration: Relief, climate, soil, water, transport, jobs, safety, and services explain why population clusters or remains sparse across regions.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one dense or sparse region through physical and economic factors.

scenes.title: Add migration and transition
scenes.objective: Connect spatial movement with demographic change.
scenes.narration: Trace one migration chain from origin push to destination pull. Then connect birth rate, death rate, natural increase, and age structure with demographic transition.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one migration chain and one demographic-transition relationship.

scenes.title: Reject swapped indicators
scenes.objective: Prepare for density, growth, fertility, migration, and distribution traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may use a correct population term in the wrong relationship. Check whether it describes a ratio, map pattern, rate of change, birth behavior, or movement.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct swapped-indicator statement.

scenes.title: Explain one population pattern
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one human-geography chain.
scenes.narration: Choose one population pattern. State the indicator, physical factor, economic factor, migration or transition link, map example, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one population pattern and reject one indicator swap.

geographyDay15PopulationStages.label: Separate the indicators
geographyDay15PopulationStages.explanation: Population density is a ratio, while distribution is a spatial pattern. Growth rate, fertility, age structure, and migration answer different questions.
geographyDay15PopulationStages.proof: Start cleanly: density, distribution, growth, fertility, and migration are related but not interchangeable.

geographyDay15PopulationStages.label: Explain concentration
geographyDay15PopulationStages.explanation: Relief, climate, soil, water, transport, jobs, safety, and services explain why people cluster in some regions and avoid others.
geographyDay15PopulationStages.proof: Map chain: physical base + access + livelihood + safety shape population distribution.

geographyDay15PopulationStages.label: Trace migration
geographyDay15PopulationStages.explanation: Migration can be seasonal, forced, voluntary, rural-urban, inter-state, or international. Always identify origin pressure, destination pull, and the resulting change.
geographyDay15PopulationStages.proof: Migration logic: origin push -> route -> destination pull -> demographic and settlement effect.

geographyDay15PopulationStages.label: Read demographic transition
geographyDay15PopulationStages.explanation: Demographic transition connects birth rate, death rate, natural increase, age structure, health, education, urbanization, and development stage.
geographyDay15PopulationStages.proof: Trap: falling fertility does not mean migration, age structure, or total population stop mattering.

geographyDay15PopulationStages.label: Reject indicator swaps
geographyDay15PopulationStages.explanation: A strong answer explains one population pattern through physical factor, economic factor, migration factor, demographic indicator, map example, and one swapped-indicator trap.
geographyDay15PopulationStages.proof: Recall chain: indicator -> factor -> map pattern -> migration or transition -> reject the swap.
