lessons.title: Settlements
lessons.promise: Classify rural and urban settlements through site, situation, morphology, function, hierarchy, connectivity, and growth pressure.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native settlement-pattern visual.

scenes.title: Separate site and situation
scenes.objective: Use precise location language before classifying settlement form.
scenes.narration: Site is the exact ground occupied by a settlement. Situation is its wider relation with routes, rivers, markets, resources, relief, and nearby regions.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can distinguish one settlement site from its situation.

scenes.title: Explain rural and urban patterns
scenes.objective: Connect settlement form with geography, land use, services, and connectivity.
scenes.narration: Rural patterns respond to relief, water, agriculture, landholding, and safety. Urban hierarchy grows through services, markets, administration, industry, and transport.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one rural pattern and one urban hierarchy relationship.

scenes.title: Read morphology and function
scenes.objective: Separate visible layout from settlement role and scale.
scenes.narration: Compact, linear, dispersed, radial, grid, and planned layouts describe morphology. Function describes role, while hierarchy describes relative level and service reach.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can classify one settlement by morphology, function, and hierarchy.

scenes.title: Reject classification swaps
scenes.objective: Prepare for site-situation, morphology-function, and hierarchy-scale traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may correctly describe a settlement feature but attach it to the wrong classification term. Check what the term is actually measuring.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct settlement classification statement.

scenes.title: Explain one settlement
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one complete settlement chain.
scenes.narration: Choose one settlement example. State its site, situation, morphology, function, hierarchy, growth pressure, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one settlement and reject one classification swap.

geographyDay16SettlementsStages.label: Separate site and situation
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.explanation: Site is the exact ground occupied by a settlement. Situation is its wider relationship with rivers, routes, markets, resources, relief, and nearby regions.
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.proof: Start cleanly: site is the place itself; situation is the wider spatial relationship.

geographyDay16SettlementsStages.label: Explain rural patterns
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.explanation: Relief, water, landholding, safety, agriculture, and community structure influence compact, dispersed, linear, and other rural settlement patterns.
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.proof: Map chain: physical base + land use + water + safety shape rural form.

geographyDay16SettlementsStages.label: Build urban hierarchy
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.explanation: Urban hierarchy moves from small towns to metropolitan regions through services, markets, administration, industry, transport, and connectivity.
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.proof: Hierarchy check: size alone is incomplete without function, service reach, and connectivity.

geographyDay16SettlementsStages.label: Read settlement morphology
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.explanation: Compact, linear, dispersed, radial, grid, and planned forms describe visible layout. Function explains what the settlement does, not only how it looks.
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.proof: Trap: morphology describes shape; function describes role; hierarchy describes relative level.

geographyDay16SettlementsStages.label: Reject cross-matched terms
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.explanation: A strong answer links one settlement with site, situation, morphology, function, hierarchy, Indian example, growth pressure, and one near-correct classification trap.
geographyDay16SettlementsStages.proof: Recall chain: site -> situation -> morphology -> function -> hierarchy -> reject the swap.

lessons.title: Economic Activities
lessons.promise: Explain primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary, and quinary activities through inputs, location factors, linkages, technology, markets, and structural change.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native sector-transition visual.

scenes.title: Begin with sector classification
scenes.objective: Separate extraction, transformation, services, knowledge, and high-order decisions.
scenes.narration: Primary activities use natural resources, secondary activities transform material, tertiary activities provide services, and quaternary or quinary activities organize knowledge and decisions.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can classify one example in each major sector group.

scenes.title: Connect the sectors
scenes.objective: Explain how an activity chain moves from resource input to market-facing value.
scenes.narration: Connect resource extraction with processing, transport, trade, finance, information, and decision systems. Real economies rely on linked sectors rather than isolated boxes.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can trace one product or service through at least three linked sectors.

scenes.title: Explain location and structural shift
scenes.objective: Connect sector geography with changing development conditions.
scenes.narration: Location still matters through resources, energy, labor, routes, markets, skills, networks, data, and policy. Productivity, urbanization, education, infrastructure, and technology change the sector mix over time.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why one sector grows or relocates as development conditions change.

scenes.title: Reject overlap traps
scenes.objective: Prepare for mixed extraction, processing, service, and knowledge examples.
scenes.narration: One economic chain can contain several sectors. Classify the specific activity being described instead of labeling the entire chain with one category.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct sector classification statement.

scenes.title: Explain one economic chain
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one sector-transition explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one activity. State the sector, input, location factor, technology or market link, development shift, and one overlap trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one sector chain and reject one classification overlap.

geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.label: Begin with extraction
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.explanation: Primary activities extract or use natural resources through agriculture, fishing, forestry, mining, and related work. Location follows the resource base.
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.proof: Start with input: primary activity depends directly on land, water, forest, or mineral base.

geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.label: Add transformation
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.explanation: Secondary activities transform raw material into goods. Industry adds energy, labor, capital, transport, technology, market, and policy to the location decision.
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.proof: Chain: resource input -> processing -> infrastructure -> market-facing output.

geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.label: Connect services
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.explanation: Tertiary activities provide services such as transport, trade, finance, health, education, and communication. They support and reorganize the other sectors.
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.proof: Trap: a service link may support production without becoming secondary activity itself.

geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.label: Add knowledge and decisions
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.explanation: Quaternary work organizes knowledge, research, data, and innovation. Quinary work emphasizes high-order decisions, leadership, and advanced public or private services.
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.proof: Location still matters through skills, networks, data, institutions, and policy.

geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.label: Explain structural change
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.explanation: As productivity, urbanization, education, infrastructure, technology, and markets change, the employment and output mix shifts while sector linkages remain interdependent.
geographyDay17EconomicActivitiesStages.proof: Recall chain: input -> transformation -> service -> knowledge -> structural shift -> reject overlap trap.

lessons.title: Transport and Trade
lessons.promise: Explain connectivity through networks, transport modes, ports, hinterland, corridors, trade flow, regional change, and map discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native connectivity visual.

scenes.title: Begin with network logic
scenes.objective: Explain how nodes and routes reduce friction of distance.
scenes.narration: Nodes, routes, junctions, corridors, and terminals reorganize access, specialization, trade flow, and regional growth.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one network as a node-route-corridor chain.

scenes.title: Compare modes and gateways
scenes.objective: Connect transport-mode advantage with port and hinterland geography.
scenes.narration: Railways, highways, waterways, air routes, and pipelines offer different advantages. Ports become gateways through harbor conditions, hinterland, corridor links, cargo, and security.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one mode advantage and one port-hinterland relationship.

scenes.title: Trace regional transformation
scenes.objective: Connect connectivity with markets, settlement, industry, and pressure.
scenes.narration: Connectivity changes specialization, market access, settlement, industry, and agriculture. It can integrate regions while also creating corridor-led inequality and ecological pressure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one corridor or port with one gain and one regional pressure.

scenes.title: Reject corridor and port mismatches
scenes.objective: Prepare for route, location, hinterland, and mode-advantage traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may use a real port, corridor, cargo, or transport advantage with the wrong hinterland, location, or route. Verify the complete map chain.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct corridor-port or mode-advantage statement.

scenes.title: Explain one connectivity chain
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one corridor, port, or network explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one corridor, port, or network. State location, transport mode, hinterland, economic effect, regional pressure, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one connectivity chain and reject one map mismatch.

geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.label: Begin with network logic
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.explanation: Transport networks reduce friction of distance. Nodes, routes, junctions, corridors, and terminals reorganize access, specialization, and regional growth.
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.proof: Start with the map: node -> route -> corridor -> market access -> regional effect.

geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.label: Compare transport modes
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.explanation: Railways, highways, waterways, air routes, and pipelines carry different cost, terrain, speed, volume, flexibility, and infrastructure advantages.
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.proof: Trap: the best mode depends on cargo, distance, terrain, urgency, and network connection.

geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.label: Connect ports and hinterland
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.explanation: A port is meaningful through its location, harbor conditions, hinterland, corridor links, cargo profile, security, and relation with domestic and global trade routes.
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.proof: Map chain: port -> hinterland -> corridor -> cargo flow -> regional transformation.

geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.label: Add regional change
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.explanation: Connectivity can integrate markets, attract industry, shift settlement, and widen opportunity while also creating corridor-led inequality and ecological pressure.
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.proof: UPSC balance: connectivity can generate growth and uneven regional consequences together.

geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.label: Reject map mismatches
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.explanation: A strong answer links one corridor, port, or network with location, mode advantage, hinterland, economic effect, regional pressure, and one swapped-pair trap.
geographyDay18TransportTradeStages.proof: Recall chain: node -> route -> mode -> hinterland -> effect -> reject the mismatch.

lessons.title: Industry Location
lessons.promise: Explain industrial location through classical factors, modern factors, old regions, new clusters, map logic, and factor-example discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native industry-location visual.

scenes.title: Begin with the dominant factor
scenes.objective: Explain why one industrial factor pulls a plant toward a location.
scenes.narration: Raw material, power, water, labor, transport, market, and capital do not pull equally. Start with the factor that most strongly changes production cost or access.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the dominant classical factor for one industry.

scenes.title: Add modern location logic
scenes.objective: Compare resource pull with skill, data, logistics, policy, and value-chain pull.
scenes.narration: Modern industry can follow skilled labor, innovation, data, supplier networks, logistics, policy, and global value chains. The strongest pull depends on the industry.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why a modern cluster may not follow a coalfield or raw-material base.

scenes.title: Compare old regions and new clusters
scenes.objective: Connect changing factors with changing industrial geography.
scenes.narration: Old regions often preserve coal, ore, rail, port, or colonial-market geography. New clusters add highways, airports, skills, suppliers, finance, policy, and agglomeration.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can compare one old industrial region with one newer cluster.

scenes.title: Reject factor-example mismatches
scenes.objective: Prepare for near-correct industry, region, and location-factor traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may use a real industry and a real factor while connecting them incorrectly. Verify the dominant factor, region, network, and changing context together.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one old-factor and new-cluster mismatch.

scenes.title: Explain one industrial location chain
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one factor-to-region explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one industry. State its dominant factor, support factors, region, network, changing context, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one industry-location chain and reject one mismatch.

geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.label: Begin with classical factors
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.explanation: Traditional industrial location weighs raw material, power, water, labor, transport, market, and capital. The dominant cost decides which factor pulls the plant most strongly.
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.proof: Start with the question: which factor creates the strongest location pull for this industry?

geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.label: Add modern factors
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.explanation: Modern industries also follow skilled labor, innovation, data, logistics, policy support, and global value chains. Weightless inputs can change the location logic.
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.proof: Trap: every industry is not tied to bulky raw material or coalfield proximity.

geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.label: Read old industrial regions
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.explanation: Older industrial regions often reflect coal, iron ore, railways, ports, water, and colonial-market links. Historical infrastructure can preserve a region after its original advantage weakens.
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.proof: Map chain: resource base -> transport link -> industry -> old industrial region.

geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.label: Explain new clusters
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.explanation: New clusters can form around services, electronics, automobiles, petrochemicals, or IT through skills, suppliers, ports, highways, finance, policy, and agglomeration benefits.
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.proof: Compare one old region with one new cluster before answering a location question.

geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.label: Reject factor mismatches
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.explanation: A near-correct UPSC statement may pair a real industry with the wrong dominant factor, region, input, port link, or cluster advantage. Test the complete location chain.
geographyDay19IndustryLocationStages.proof: Recall chain: industry -> dominant factor -> region -> network -> reject the mismatch.

scenes.objective: Explain why one industrial factor pulls a plant toward a location.
scenes.narration: Raw material, power, water, labor, transport, market, and capital do not pull equally. Start with the factor that most strongly changes production cost or access.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the dominant classical factor for one industry.

lessons.title: Regional Development
lessons.promise: Explain regional inequality through spatial gaps, planning regions, urban pressure, governance response, sustainability, and solution discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native geography-governance visual.

scenes.title: Begin with the regional gap
scenes.objective: Explain development as an uneven spatial pattern.
scenes.narration: Resource base, location, connectivity, human capital, and institutions differ across regions. Start by naming the gap and the indicator that reveals it.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one regional disparity with one indicator and one geographic cause.

scenes.title: Match the planning unit
scenes.objective: Choose the region type that fits the development problem.
scenes.narration: A planning region may be functional, administrative, resource-based, river-basin based, or problem-region based. Choose the boundary that fits the issue.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can match one development issue with one suitable planning region.

scenes.title: Balance growth and pressure
scenes.objective: Connect urbanization and growth poles with regional opportunity and stress.
scenes.narration: Growth centers can create jobs, services, and innovation while also intensifying housing, transport, waste, water, pollution, and peri-urban pressure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state one growth-pole gain and one sustainability pressure.

scenes.title: Reject cause-solution mismatches
scenes.objective: Prepare for disparity, indicator, region, and policy traps.
scenes.narration: A policy is not correct merely because it sounds useful. Verify the spatial cause, indicator, planning unit, governance capacity, and ecological limit together.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct regional-development solution.

scenes.title: Explain one regional development chain
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one gap-to-response explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one region or urban issue. State the gap, cause, indicator, planning unit, response, sustainability risk, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one regional development chain and reject one mismatch.

scenes.title: Explain one regional development chain
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one gap-to-response explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one region or urban issue. State the gap, cause, indicator, planning unit, response, sustainability risk, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one regional development chain and reject one mismatch.

geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.label: Begin with spatial inequality
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.explanation: Regional development differs because resource base, location, connectivity, human capital, and institutions differ. Development is a spatial pattern, not one national average.
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.proof: Begin with the map: where is the gap, which indicator shows it, and what creates it?

geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.label: Choose the planning region
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.explanation: Planning regions can be functional, administrative, resource-based, river-basin based, or problem-region based. The correct unit depends on the issue being solved.
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.proof: Trap: a useful administrative boundary is not always the best planning boundary.

geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.label: Read growth and urban stress
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.explanation: Urbanization can create jobs, services, and innovation while also intensifying housing, transport, waste, water, pollution, and peri-urban pressure.
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.proof: Balance the answer: growth pole -> opportunity -> pressure -> sustainability response.

geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.label: Connect geography and governance
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.explanation: Backwardness and growth poles must be read through both geography and governance. Infrastructure, institutions, policy capacity, participation, and ecological limits shape the outcome.
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.proof: A policy response must fit the location factor, indicator, planning unit, and sustainability risk.

geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.label: Reject solution mismatches
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.explanation: A near-correct UPSC statement may confuse cause, indicator, planning unit, growth-pole effect, or policy solution. Verify each link before accepting the claim.
geographyDay20RegionalDevelopmentStages.proof: Recall chain: gap -> cause -> indicator -> region -> response -> reject the mismatch.

lessons.title: Human Geography Consolidation
lessons.promise: Integrate population, settlements, economic activity, connectivity, industry, regional development, and weak-link repair in one map-cause framework.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Human and Economic Geography pack into a compact portal-native Week 3 consolidation visual.

scenes.title: Begin with people and place
scenes.objective: Connect population distribution with opportunity and constraint.
scenes.narration: People concentrate through relief, water, climate, safety, jobs, services, and historical movement. Begin with the map before naming the settlement outcome.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one population pattern with one physical base and one human factor.

scenes.title: Connect settlement and activity
scenes.objective: Trace how jobs and services reshape settlement patterns.
scenes.narration: Rural forms, towns, cities, service centers, and urban hierarchies interact with agriculture, industry, services, knowledge work, and trade.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one activity chain and its settlement effect.

scenes.title: Add networks, industry, and region
scenes.objective: Integrate transport, cluster, policy, and regional-development logic.
scenes.narration: Corridors, ports, markets, industrial clusters, policy, and governance alter access, specialization, growth, pressure, and disparity.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can trace one network-to-region chain with one gain and one pressure.

scenes.title: Reject integrated mismatches
scenes.objective: Prepare for mixed-sector, location, indicator, and policy traps.
scenes.narration: A statement can combine correct population, settlement, sector, corridor, industry, and policy facts in the wrong relationship. Verify the full chain.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct mixed human-geography statement.

scenes.title: Repair the weakest link
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one integrated explanation and one repair card.
scenes.narration: Choose one chain. State people, settlement, economic activity, network or industry, regional outcome, pressure, weak link, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one integrated chain and name one weak-link repair.

geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.label: Begin with people and place
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.explanation: Population distribution reflects relief, water, climate, safety, jobs, services, and historical movement. Begin the integrated answer with where people concentrate and why.
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.proof: Start with the map: people -> physical base -> opportunity or constraint.

geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.label: Connect settlements
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.explanation: Settlements translate population patterns into rural forms, towns, cities, service centers, and urban hierarchies. Site, situation, function, and connectivity work together.
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.proof: Trap: settlement pattern, settlement function, and urban hierarchy are related but not interchangeable.

geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.label: Trace the activity chain
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.explanation: Resources, agriculture, industry, services, knowledge work, and trade form overlapping activity chains. Structural change alters jobs, settlement, and regional opportunity.
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.proof: Explain one chain: input -> activity -> job pattern -> settlement effect.

geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.label: Add networks and regions
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.explanation: Transport, ports, corridors, industrial clusters, markets, policy, and governance reshape regional development. Physical location and human decision logic must remain connected.
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.proof: Map chain: network -> industry or service -> regional gain -> pressure or disparity.

geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.label: Generate repair cards
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.explanation: An integrated answer is only as strong as its weakest link. Record the missing indicator, example, map link, or mismatch trap before moving into mixed MCQs.
geographyDay21HumanGeographyConsolidatio.proof: Recall chain: people -> settlement -> economy -> network -> region -> repair the weak link.

lessons.title: Atlas Mastery
lessons.promise: Convert atlas reading into exam recall through orientation, neighbors, layered maps, quick context, and nearby-location trap discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Atlas, PYQ Logic, Revision pack into a compact portal-native map-recall visual.

scenes.title: Begin with orientation
scenes.objective: Place a recurring location inside one direction and region.
scenes.narration: Atlas mastery is not memorizing every label. Begin with direction, region, and one stable location anchor before adding detail.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can locate one recurring place through direction and region.

scenes.title: Attach neighbors and layers
scenes.objective: Convert an isolated label into relational map memory.
scenes.narration: Attach neighboring states, countries, rivers, ranges, coasts, or seas. Then add one relief, river, climate, resource, biodiversity, port, border, or current-affairs layer.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one location with one neighbor, one physical feature, and one layer.

scenes.title: Build a quick recall drill
scenes.objective: Move from map location to significance without scanning the whole atlas.
scenes.narration: Recall the location, connect its region and feature, explain why it matters, and compare one nearby place that could become a distractor.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can run the locate-connect-explain-compare drill for one place.

scenes.title: Reject regional swaps
scenes.objective: Prepare for nearby-location and feature-pair traps.
scenes.narration: A statement may use real neighboring places, rivers, ranges, ports, or biodiversity locations while swapping the relationship. Verify the complete map chain.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can reject one near-correct nearby-location statement.

scenes.title: Explain one atlas location
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one context-rich map explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one location. State direction, neighboring area, physical feature, human or current link, one comparison, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one atlas location and reject one regional swap.

geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.label: Begin with orientation
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.explanation: Atlas mastery begins with direction and region. A location becomes useful only when the student can place it north, south, east, west, coastal, inland, mountain, plateau, plain, or border-side.
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.proof: Start with the map: direction -> region -> location.

geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.label: Attach neighboring areas
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.explanation: Every recurring UPSC location should carry nearby regions, states, countries, rivers, ranges, coasts, or seas. Relationship memory is more reliable than an isolated label.
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.proof: Map chain: location -> neighbor -> physical feature -> regional context.

geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.label: Read layered maps
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.explanation: Revise India and world maps through layers: relief, rivers, climate, resources, biodiversity, ports, borders, and current-affairs locations. Each layer answers a different kind of question.
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.proof: Do not memorize a label alone: attach one physical layer and one human or current link.

geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.label: Build quick recall
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.explanation: Recall becomes faster when a place is tied to cause and region. Move from location to feature, significance, current relevance, and one likely comparison instead of scanning the whole atlas.
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.proof: Quick drill: locate -> connect -> explain -> compare.

geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.label: Reject nearby-location swaps
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.explanation: A near-correct UPSC statement often uses two nearby regions or real features and swaps their relationship. Verify direction, neighbor, layer, and significance together.
geographyDay22AtlasMasteryStages.proof: Recall chain: direction -> neighbor -> layer -> link -> reject the regional swap.

lessons.title: PYQ Pattern Reading
lessons.promise: Read Geography PYQs through tested idea, process order, map location, pair matching, exceptions, explanation validity, and repair-card discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Atlas, PYQ Logic, Revision pack into a compact portal-native trap-reading visual.

scenes.title: Classify the tested idea
scenes.objective: Separate content difficulty from question-design difficulty.
scenes.narration: Before solving, identify whether UPSC is testing concept, map, process order, exception, pair matching, or a current-static link.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can classify one PYQ-style statement by trap type.

scenes.title: Verify order, location, pair, and exception
scenes.objective: Inspect the relationship that can turn a true fact into a false option.
scenes.narration: A familiar fact can be attached to the wrong process stage, region, season, soil, crop, current, or hazard. Test each pair and exception independently.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why one near-correct statement fails.

scenes.title: Test explanation validity
scenes.objective: Check whether the claimed cause actually produces the effect.
scenes.narration: Two statements may both be true while their explanatory relationship remains false. Verify mechanism, place, timing, and direction of effect.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate two true facts from a valid cause-effect link.

scenes.title: Reject the tempting option
scenes.objective: Prepare for familiar-wording and mixed-relationship traps.
scenes.narration: Reject an option when the tested relationship fails, even if every noun inside the statement looks familiar. Name the precise mismatch.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the exact mismatch inside one tempting option.

scenes.title: Turn error into repair
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one trap explanation and one corrected statement.
scenes.narration: Choose one PYQ-style trap. State what is tested, why the option is tempting, why it fails, the corrected statement, and the retest cue.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one trap and create one repair card.

scenes.narration: Before solving, identify whether UPSC is testing concept, map, process order, exception, pair matching, or a current-static link.

scenes.objective: Prepare for familiar-wording and mixed-relationship traps.

scenes.narration: Choose one PYQ-style trap. State what is tested, why the option is tempting, why it fails, the corrected statement, and the retest cue.

geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.label: Classify the tested idea
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.explanation: PYQ pattern reading separates difficulty from trap design. First identify whether UPSC is testing a concept, map location, process order, exception, pair match, or current-static link.
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.proof: Start with the question: what is actually being tested here?

geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.label: Verify process and location
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.explanation: A correct fact becomes wrong when attached to the wrong stage, region, season, current, soil, crop, or hazard. Verify the process order or map anchor before accepting the statement.
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.proof: Trap check: right fact + wrong order or location = wrong statement.

geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.label: Inspect pairs and exceptions
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.explanation: Pair-matching questions mix familiar names with one weak relationship. Exception questions hide the single statement that breaks the pattern. Test each pair independently.
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.proof: Do not accept a group because most entries look familiar.

geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.label: Test cause and effect
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.explanation: Two statements can both be true while the explanation remains false. Check whether the claimed cause actually produces the stated effect in that region, season, or process.
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.proof: Reason test: true statement A + true statement B does not guarantee a valid link.

geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.label: Create a repair card
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.explanation: Tag the error by trap type and rewrite the false statement correctly. Revision should repair the thinking error, not only store the answer key.
geographyDay23PyqPatternReadingStages.proof: Recall chain: classify -> verify -> reject -> rewrite -> retest.

lessons.title: Disaster Geography Bridge
lessons.promise: Connect physical geography with disaster management through hazard, exposure, vulnerability, capacity, response, and UPSC mismatch discipline.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Atlas, PYQ Logic, Revision pack into a compact portal-native hazard-to-risk visual.

scenes.title: Separate hazard from disaster
scenes.objective: Begin with physical mechanism before discussing damage.
scenes.narration: A cyclone, flood, drought, landslide, or earthquake is a physical hazard. It becomes a disaster when exposure, vulnerability, and weak capacity combine.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate one hazard mechanism from its disaster outcome.

scenes.title: Locate exposure and vulnerability
scenes.objective: Explain why the same hazard produces unequal damage.
scenes.narration: Trace people, infrastructure, land use, housing, slopes, drainage, ecosystems, and livelihoods inside the affected region before comparing outcomes.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify one exposed asset and one vulnerability factor.

scenes.title: Add mitigation and institutions
scenes.objective: Connect geography with preparedness and governance.
scenes.narration: Use early warning, evacuation, resilient infrastructure, land-use planning, ecosystem protection, relief coordination, and local preparedness as risk-reduction tools.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can pair one mitigation tool with a responsible institution or policy link.

scenes.title: Reject the cross-matched option
scenes.objective: Prepare for near-correct disaster-geography statements.
scenes.narration: Verify hazard cause, vulnerable region, mitigation tool, and institution independently. Reject the statement when one relationship is cross-matched.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the precise mismatch inside one tempting option.

scenes.title: Explain one regional disaster
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one complete hazard-to-risk chain.
scenes.narration: Choose one disaster. State the hazard mechanism, vulnerable region, exposure, vulnerability, mitigation tool, institution or policy link, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one disaster through a complete regional risk chain.

geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.label: Name the physical hazard
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.explanation: A cyclone, flood, drought, landslide, or earthquake begins as a physical hazard. First explain the process and locate the region where that process is plausible.
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.proof: Hazard is the physical event. Disaster is the human outcome.

geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.label: Locate what is exposed
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.explanation: The same hazard produces different consequences when settlements, farms, roads, ports, power lines, or critical services occupy the affected area.
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.proof: Exposure check: what lies in the path of the hazard?

geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.label: Explain unequal vulnerability
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.explanation: Housing quality, slope cutting, drainage, land use, poverty, ecosystem loss, and fragile infrastructure change how severely an exposed region is affected.
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.proof: Do not treat two exposed regions as equally vulnerable.

geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.label: Add capacity and response
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.explanation: Early warning, evacuation, resilient infrastructure, land-use planning, ecosystem protection, relief coordination, and local preparedness reduce disaster risk.
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.proof: Capacity check: identify one mitigation tool and one responsible institution.

geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.label: Reject the mismatch
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.explanation: A statement can contain real terms while pairing the wrong hazard cause, vulnerable region, mitigation tool, or institution. Verify every relationship.
geographyDay24DisasterGeographyBridgeSta.proof: Recall chain: hazard -> exposure -> vulnerability -> capacity -> verify.

geographyDay24PortalLesson.title: Disaster Geography Bridge
geographyDay24PortalLesson.promise: Connect physical geography with disaster management through hazard, exposure, vulnerability, capacity, response, and UPSC mismatch discipline.
geographyDay24PortalLesson.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Atlas, PYQ Logic, Revision pack into a compact portal-native hazard-to-risk visual.

scenes.title: Separate hazard from disaster
scenes.objective: Begin with physical mechanism before discussing damage.
scenes.narration: A cyclone, flood, drought, landslide, or earthquake is a physical hazard. It becomes a disaster when exposure, vulnerability, and weak capacity combine.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate one hazard mechanism from its disaster outcome.

scenes.title: Locate exposure and vulnerability
scenes.objective: Explain why the same hazard produces unequal damage.
scenes.narration: Trace people, infrastructure, land use, housing, slopes, drainage, ecosystems, and livelihoods inside the affected region before comparing outcomes.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify one exposed asset and one vulnerability factor.

scenes.title: Add mitigation and institutions
scenes.objective: Connect geography with preparedness and governance.
scenes.narration: Use early warning, evacuation, resilient infrastructure, land-use planning, ecosystem protection, relief coordination, and local preparedness as risk-reduction tools.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can pair one mitigation tool with a responsible institution or policy link.

scenes.title: Reject the cross-matched option
scenes.objective: Prepare for near-correct disaster-geography statements.
scenes.narration: Verify hazard cause, vulnerable region, mitigation tool, and institution independently. Reject the statement when one relationship is cross-matched.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the precise mismatch inside one tempting option.

scenes.title: Explain one regional disaster

lessons.promise: Connect geography with environment through biome controls, habitat logic, biodiversity patterns, climate exposure, conservation regions, and cross-match discipline.

scenes.title: Begin with biome controls
scenes.objective: Use climate and location as the base layer for environment questions.
scenes.narration: Temperature, rainfall, seasonality, latitude, altitude, and soil shape biomes. Begin with those controls before naming vegetation, species, or protected areas.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one biome through its climate and location controls.

scenes.title: Trace habitat and biodiversity
scenes.objective: Connect species patterns with plausible ecological geography.
scenes.narration: Use habitat, corridors, endemism, climate gradients, and human pressure to explain biodiversity distribution before accepting a species-location pair.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why one species or biodiversity pattern belongs in a region.

scenes.title: Locate exposure and conservation
scenes.objective: Read climate risk and conservation response spatially.
scenes.narration: Compare coasts, mountains, drylands, forests, islands, and urban zones through exposure. Then attach habitat, species, state, basin, range, or coast to one conservation region.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can locate one climate-sensitive region and one conservation map cue.

scenes.title: Reject the cross-matched option
scenes.objective: Prepare for near-correct environment-geography statements.
scenes.narration: Verify species, habitat, protected area, state, and climate zone independently. Reject the statement when one relationship is cross-matched.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the precise mismatch inside one tempting environment option.

scenes.title: Explain one environment-geography link
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one complete map-linked explanation.
scenes.narration: Choose one biome, habitat, species, climate-risk region, or conservation area. State the geographic control, map cue, ecological link, and one UPSC trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one environment question through geography.

geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.label: Read the biome controls
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.explanation: Biomes emerge through temperature, rainfall, seasonality, latitude, altitude, and soil. Start with the geographic controls before naming vegetation or species.
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.proof: Biome check: climate pattern first, ecological label second.

geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.label: Trace biodiversity patterns
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.explanation: Biodiversity depends on habitat, corridors, endemism, climate gradients, and human pressure. Species questions become easier when the habitat logic is mapped first.
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.proof: Ask why a species range is plausible in that habitat.

geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.label: Locate climate exposure
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.explanation: Coasts, mountains, drylands, forests, islands, and urban zones experience different climate-change pressures. Name the exposed region and the mechanism.
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.proof: Exposure check: which geography makes the region sensitive?

geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.label: Map conservation regions
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.explanation: Read a conservation region through habitat, species, state, river basin, mountain range, or coast before attaching a protected-area or policy label.
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.proof: Map cue: region -> habitat -> species -> conservation response.

geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.label: Reject the cross-match
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.explanation: A statement can contain real species, habitats, protected areas, states, and climate zones while pairing them incorrectly. Verify each relationship.
geographyDay25EnvironmentGeographyBridge.proof: Recall chain: biome -> habitat -> exposure -> region -> verify.

lessons.title: Mains Geography Application
lessons.promise: Convert Geography knowledge into a clear 10-marker through context, mechanism, map or diagram, example, and balanced conclusion.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Atlas, PYQ Logic, Revision pack into a compact portal-native answer-writing visual.

scenes.title: Build the 10-marker skeleton
scenes.objective: Create a reliable answer structure before adding facts.
scenes.narration: Open with definition or context. Then move through mechanism, map or diagram, example, and a balanced conclusion. Let each part serve the argument.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state the five-part answer skeleton without reading the slide.

scenes.title: Convert content into causal flow
scenes.objective: Explain how and why instead of listing fragments.
scenes.narration: Use cause, process, and consequence. Connect the concept through a short logical chain before inserting supporting facts.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can turn one topic into a causal explanation.

scenes.title: Use spatial proof
scenes.objective: Choose a visual that clarifies the answer.
scenes.narration: Add a locator map, flow diagram, cross-section, or compact comparison only when it improves comprehension. Then attach one location-specific example.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can choose one useful visual and one evidence-rich example.

scenes.title: Avoid the fact dump
scenes.objective: Prepare for weak-answer habits.
scenes.narration: Correct facts do not rescue an answer that lacks causal flow, spatial evidence, example discipline, or conclusion. Identify and remove the loose list.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why one fact-heavy answer remains weak.

scenes.title: Pitch one complete answer
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one mains-ready outline.
scenes.narration: Choose one Geography topic. State the intro, causal mechanism, map or diagram, example, balanced conclusion, and one trap that would weaken the answer.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one complete 10-marker outline.

geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.label: Frame the answer
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.explanation: Begin with a definition or context line, then organize the answer through mechanism, spatial proof, example, and a balanced conclusion.
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.proof: Answer frame: context -> mechanism -> spatial proof -> example -> conclusion.

geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.label: Explain the causal flow
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.explanation: A geography answer needs cause, process, and consequence. Use a short causal chain so the examiner can see how one variable changes another.
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.proof: Mechanism check: show how and why, not only what.

geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.label: Add a useful visual
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.explanation: Use a locator map, flow diagram, cross-section, or small comparison table only when it clarifies the argument. The visual must earn its space.
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.proof: Visual check: the map or diagram must explain, not decorate.

geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.label: Anchor one example
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.explanation: Add one India or world example with location, cause, impact, and a policy or way-forward cue where relevant. Specific evidence strengthens the explanation.
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.proof: Example check: place + process + impact + response.

geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.label: Reject the fact dump
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.explanation: A long list of correct facts still produces a weak answer when causal flow, spatial evidence, examples, and conclusion discipline are missing.
geographyDay26MainsGeographyApplicationS.proof: Recall chain: frame -> explain -> visualize -> anchor -> conclude.

scenes.title: Start from the physical base
scenes.objective: Recall the mechanisms that support the entire subject.
scenes.narration: Move quickly through structure, relief, external processes, atmosphere, climate, and oceans. Name the process, location logic, and consequence.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state one physical-geography chain without isolated memorization.

scenes.title: Overlay India and human geography
scenes.objective: Connect spatial patterns with people, activities, and networks.
scenes.narration: Attach India relief, drainage, monsoon, soils, resources, and agriculture before tracing population, settlements, sectors, corridors, industry, and development outcomes.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can connect one India map pattern with one human-geography outcome.

scenes.title: Bridge environment and disaster
scenes.objective: Cross chapter boundaries through one integrated explanation.
scenes.narration: Add habitat, biodiversity, climate exposure, hazard, vulnerability, and capacity. Integrated questions commonly combine one process, one location, and one impact.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain one environment or disaster link through the geographic base.

scenes.title: Reject the wrong chapter context
scenes.objective: Prepare for mixed-drill statement traps.
scenes.narration: A fact can be correct while the chapter relationship is wrong. Verify process, place, impact, and context before accepting an integrated statement.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the precise wrong-context mismatch inside one option.

scenes.title: Create one repair card
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one integrated weak-area diagnosis.
scenes.narration: Choose one weak answer. Tag chapter, map zone, concept type, and trap type. Add cause, map cue, correction, and fresh retest plan.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one integrated chain and one repair card.

geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.label: Recall the physical base
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.explanation: Begin with structure, relief, weathering, atmosphere, climate, and oceans. Physical mechanisms create the base layer for India, human, environment, and disaster geography.

geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.label: Overlay the India map
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.explanation: Attach relief, drainage, monsoon, climate, soil, vegetation, resources, agriculture, and regional examples to the physical mechanism.
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.proof: India map check: locate before accepting a familiar statement.

geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.label: Connect people and networks
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.explanation: Trace population, settlement, economic activity, transport, industry, and regional development through resources, services, networks, and governance.
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.proof: Human chain: people -> activity -> network -> regional outcome.

geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.label: Add environment and disaster
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.explanation: Link habitat, biodiversity, climate exposure, hazard, vulnerability, and capacity to the geographic base. Integrated questions cross chapter boundaries.
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.proof: Bridge check: one process + one location + one impact.

geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.label: Build the weak-area heatmap
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.explanation: Tag each wrong answer by chapter, map zone, concept type, and trap type. Turn the error into a repair card with cause, map cue, and retest.
geographyDay27FullGeographyDrillStages.proof: Recall chain: classify -> connect -> locate -> repair -> retest.

lessons.title: Weak Area Repair
lessons.promise: Repair repeated Geography mistakes through precise classification, root cause, narrow revisit cards, fresh retest, and revision scheduling.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged revision pack into a compact portal-native targeted-recovery visual.

scenes.title: Classify the mistake
scenes.objective: Separate the weak signal before revising.
scenes.narration: Identify whether the error is a knowledge gap, map-recall gap, concept confusion, or statement-reading error. Avoid random chapter-wide revision.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can classify one repeated error precisely.

scenes.title: Find root cause and repair
scenes.objective: Create one narrow revisit card.
scenes.narration: Name the unstable process, location, exception, or trap. Add concept, map cue, example, correction, and the question type that will retest it.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can write one complete repair card.

scenes.title: Retest with fresh questions
scenes.objective: Prove repair instead of repeating an answer.
scenes.narration: Use a fresh question against the repaired concept. Compare the original confidence with the retest result and keep only unresolved gaps.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why the retest is genuinely fresh.

scenes.title: Avoid chapter-wide overrevision
scenes.objective: Prepare for inefficient recovery habits.
scenes.narration: Do not reopen a whole chapter when one location, process, or reading trap remains weak. Keep the revision queue precise.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can name the smallest remaining repair target.

scenes.title: Schedule the remaining weak idea
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one repair and retest plan.
scenes.narration: Explain the mistake type, root cause, repair card, fresh retest, confidence comparison, and next revision date.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one precise recovery plan.

geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.label: Classify the mistake
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.explanation: Separate knowledge gap, map-recall gap, concept confusion, and statement-reading error before opening another chapter or question set.
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.proof: First question: what kind of mistake actually happened?

geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.label: Find the root cause
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.explanation: Repeated mistakes need root-cause repair. Identify the exact process, location, exception, or trap pattern that remains unstable.
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.proof: Root-cause check: name the smallest unstable idea.

geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.label: Write one repair card
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.explanation: Build a compact revisit card with concept, map cue, example, UPSC trap, and the corrected explanation. Keep the repair narrow.
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.proof: Repair card: concept + map cue + example + trap + correction.

geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.label: Use a fresh retest
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.explanation: Retest the repaired idea with fresh questions. Compare original confidence with retest confidence instead of repeating the memorized answer.
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.proof: Retest check: new question, same repaired concept.

geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.label: Schedule the remainder
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.explanation: Move only the remaining unstable ideas into the next revision window. Do not reopen an entire chapter when one location or process is weak.
geographyDay28WeakAreaRepairStages.proof: Recall chain: classify -> root cause -> repair -> retest -> schedule.

lessons.title: Final Mock and Review
lessons.promise: Convert the final mock into a precise repair queue through error classification, map correction, priority, retest, and confidence control.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Command Phase pack into a compact portal-native mock-analysis visual.

scenes.title: Read beyond the score
scenes.objective: Treat mock performance as evidence, not a verdict.
scenes.narration: Open every mistake. Separate content weakness from map error, statement-reading error, overconfidence, and time pressure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain why the score alone is not the diagnosis.

scenes.title: Classify and repair
scenes.objective: Give every error a category and short correction.
scenes.narration: Tag the primary error category, write the corrected concept, and attach one retest cue. Use map repair for location, direction, neighbor, feature, and cause-link errors.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can turn one wrong question into one repair action.

scenes.title: Prioritize the 24-hour queue
scenes.objective: Put repeated and high-value mistakes first.
scenes.narration: Rank mistakes by repeat risk and exam value. Schedule the smallest useful revision action before command day.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the highest-priority repair item.

scenes.title: Reject false confidence
scenes.objective: Prepare for score and familiarity traps.
scenes.narration: A familiar topic can still fail under pressure. Confidence must follow correction and fresh retest, not recognition alone.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate familiarity from proven recall.

scenes.title: Explain one mock repair
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one prioritized correction.
scenes.narration: State the mistake category, corrected idea, map or process cue, retest prompt, queue priority, and confidence decision.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one final-mock repair path.

geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.label: Read beyond the score
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.explanation: A mock score is only a signal. Open the mistakes and separate content weakness from attention, map recall, confidence, and time-pressure errors.
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.proof: Score check: the number is not the diagnosis.

geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.label: Classify every mistake
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.explanation: Tag each error as knowledge gap, map error, statement-reading error, overconfidence, or time pressure before choosing the repair.
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.proof: Classification check: one mistake, one primary category.

geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.label: Repair spatial errors
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.explanation: Target wrong locations, neighboring regions, directions, features, and cause links. Add a short visual cue that prevents the repeat error.
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.proof: Map repair: locate, compare neighbor, explain feature, retest.

geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.label: Build the 24-hour queue
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.explanation: Move repeated and high-value mistakes to the top. Give every wrong answer a short correction and one retest cue.
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.proof: Queue check: repeat risk + exam value decide priority.
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.diagramCue: repeat risk  exam value  retest cue

geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.label: Control confidence
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.explanation: Separate genuine command from familiarity. Confidence rises only when the corrected idea survives a fresh retest under realistic pressure.
geographyDay29FinalMockReviewStages.proof: Recall chain: score -> classify -> repair -> prioritize -> retest.

scenes.title: Prioritize the 24-hour queue
scenes.objective: Put repeated and high-value mistakes first.
scenes.narration: Rank mistakes by repeat risk and exam value. Schedule the smallest useful revision action before command day.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the highest-priority repair item.

scenes.title: Reject false confidence
scenes.objective: Prepare for score and familiarity traps.
scenes.narration: A familiar topic can still fail under pressure. Confidence must follow correction and fresh retest, not recognition alone.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate familiarity from proven recall.

scenes.title: Explain one mock repair
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one prioritized correction.
scenes.narration: State the mistake category, corrected idea, map or process cue, retest prompt, queue priority, and confidence decision.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain one final-mock repair path.

lessons.title: Geography Command Day
lessons.promise: Close the sprint with full recall, map confidence, proof-based command status, revision dates, and an honest next-step verdict.
lessons.sourceSummary: Promoted from the staged Command Phase pack into a compact portal-native command-day visual.

scenes.title: Run full-syllabus recall
scenes.objective: Explain every major theme through cause and location.
scenes.narration: Move across physical systems, India map, human geography, atlas, environment bridge, and disaster bridge. State mechanism, place, example, and trap.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can explain the complete subject map without recognition-only recall.

scenes.title: Audit spatial and learning proof
scenes.objective: Base confidence on evidence.
scenes.narration: Check recurring places, directions, neighboring regions, relief, rivers, climate, resources, biodiversity, and hazards. Then review Watch, Talk, MCQ, and Revisit closure.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can identify the proof behind the command decision.

scenes.title: Lock revision dates
scenes.objective: Protect retention after the sprint.
scenes.narration: Schedule the next review dates before moving to another subject. Preserve repair cards and retest cues so future revision starts from evidence.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can state the next revision date and remaining weak cue.

scenes.title: Reject familiarity as command
scenes.objective: Prepare for honest closeout.
scenes.narration: Recognizing a topic is not the same as explaining and applying it under exam pressure. Let the evidence decide the status.
scenes.checkpoint: Student can separate familiarity from proven command.

scenes.title: Set the Geography verdict
scenes.objective: Move into discussion with one honest subject closeout.
scenes.narration: State strongest area, weakest area, map confidence, retest need, revision date, and whether Geography is command-ready, revision-needed, or retest-needed.
scenes.checkpoint: Student is ready to explain the final Geography command verdict.

geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.explanation: Explain every major Geography theme through cause and location: physical systems, India map, human geography, atlas, environment bridge, and disaster bridge.

geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.explanation: Verify recurring places, directions, neighboring regions, relief, rivers, climate, resources, biodiversity, hazards, and examples one final time.
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.proof: Map check: locate, connect, compare, and explain.

geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.label: Audit the evidence
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.explanation: Use completed lessons, accepted Talk explanations, optional Lab evidence, fresh MCQ practice, and revisit closure as the confidence record.
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.proof: Proof check: Watch + Talk + MCQ + Revisit closure.
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.diagramCue: Watch  Talk  Lab support  MCQ  Revisit

geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.label: Lock revision dates
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.explanation: Schedule the next review dates before moving to another subject. Preserve the repair cards and retest queue for future command checks.
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.proof: Revision lock: the next review date is part of completion.

geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.label: Set the command verdict
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.explanation: Mark Geography command-ready, revision-needed, or retest-needed from evidence. Familiarity is not command under exam pressure.
geographyDay30GeographyCommandDayStages.proof: Recall chain: explain -> locate -> prove -> schedule -> decide.
